text: Mr. Stoffer rightly states that the deal consists of multiple elements. That is correct. Mr. Chairman, allow me to mention the following. During the presentation of the government statement, the opposition said that for them, three things are essentially guiding principles for support. That was in the context of the outstretched hand. Those were schoolbooks, school lunches, and the donation deduction in income tax. The Cabinet proactively sent this to the House. I also understand how politics works: you receive it and you look to see where you can unravel the ball of wool further. Subsequently, the VAT discussion was added to that. I heard Mr. Stoffer speak about single-income households. We held a debate on that, and there is also a proposal regarding it. I will provide an assessment of that shortly, also bearing in mind the political judgment that Mr. Stoffer attaches to it. Mr. Grinwis linked the donation deduction in corporate tax to that. There would be a majority for that with the three conventional parties, in the words of these three parties. Subsequently, Mr. Vijlbrief walked around in a dart jacket in connection with the Political Debates. He said: I am still here too; I would also like to be involved. It is smart that he is not allowing himself to be sidelined. That was clever political maneuvering by Mr. Vijlbrief. He broadened the VAT discussion. I observed: let us try to unravel that ball of wool, that spaghetti monster. I see broader support for VAT on accommodation, because that was also included in election manifestos and motions. But I also realize that it is interconnected and that we must view it in light of how we view the VAT system in general. I refer back briefly to the starting point of simplification, the shift from direct to indirect taxes, and the fiscal schemes that are inefficient and that we want to get rid of. Along those three points, it forms a prism, and along that prism, in the coming months, in the run-up to the Spring Budget, we must jointly seek a solution. I am also very willing to do so. In doing so, I also acknowledge Mr. Stoffer's point, who is performing clever political maneuvering to secure an important point for himself, just like a broadly supported Tax Plan.

Then I return to my three-pronged approach: we have a purchasing power package with a tax relief of 2 billion, excise duties are going down, and — I now come to the most important point — childcare for working people will become almost free. That really makes a significant difference for a great many working people. I will return to the long-term reforms in January and February. For now, I will make proposals shortly — I am getting ahead of myself here — to look primarily at the full-time bonus. In the Tax Plan, we have already been able to increase the work-related expenses scheme. That is also important for working people, because it ensures that they can receive, for example, a Christmas package or something extra. I will propose how we can make overtime extra attractive by reversing that system, à la the Belgian system. I want to look at the leave arrangements. And I want to look at the Working Time Discrimination Act. But I will explain that a little further in a moment, if Ms. Patijn allows me.

Let me return to that last point regarding the assessment of the motion. I hear what Ms. Van Dijk is saying, but I also see Mr. Grinwis coming running. We must therefore conduct the debate on VAT across the board. I am indeed looking for where the real drama and pain lie, which is also being pointed out by the opposition. The opposition also wondered the following: Is there arbitrariness in the demarcation issues, causing us to no longer fully follow the original reasoning regarding the simplification of fiscal regulations and the shift from direct to indirect taxes? That debate is indeed focusing on VAT on cultural goods and services. I have seen pamphlets regarding this, and I have also seen people walking around here in the hall in festive attire to draw attention to it. That is the reason I am focusing on that, but I also see Mr. Grinwis standing here. I think that, leading up to the Spring Budget, we need to conduct a broad debate on how we view VAT in general, with all the demarcation issues involved. Given the dramatic nature of the situation, I believe that the direction of the solution indeed focuses on those cultural goods and services, but let us avoid anticipating that, otherwise we will take a step back in this debate. In my opinion, we were actually moving closer to one another. Let me put the assessment down clearly on paper, so that Mr. Grinwis, Ms. Inge van Dijk, and Mr. Vijlbrief can also weigh it carefully, so that we are speaking on the same task and can form a sound judgment regarding it leading up to the vote.

I stand up for the institutions. I try to do that without responding to individual tweets. That is what I did yesterday morning, and incidentally, that is what I also did last night at the General Political Debate in the Senate. There, I actually did not comment on tweets again. I did say that I believe the mayor deserves the support of the Cabinet, just as the fundamental right to demonstrate must remain firmly intact. Every mayor has to make these kinds of difficult trade-offs and decisions every day. I have explicitly called attention to this, as I have done again now. I have also explicitly spoken out about this. I believe that is how I give substance to the Declaration of the Rule of Law. I do indeed think it is important to stand up for the institutions.

Then I leave it to the House's judgment, with reference to the debate. The motion on document no. 92 concerned safeguarding school meals in the coalition programme. Advised against for the same financial reasons. Then the motion on document no. 93 regarding not leaving the distribution decision to municipalities that do not wish to do so. Advised against. The law applies. I do not preempt steps based on the law under the motto "if then". Chair. With that I come... Oh, I had to be reminded of an important motion by Ms. Bikker. Or rather, a motion she submitted quite some time ago. I had asked on both the right and the left to remind me of it, because I knew I was going to forget that motion. That is not because it is unimportant, but because it was not entirely provided with the right paperwork. It was that simple. That concerns the deferred motion on tax system allowances. I leave the judgment on that to the House. That seems to me the only correct way to deal with it. Moreover, I have already indicated in the debate that the long-term perspective is emphatically present as far as the Cabinet is concerned.

I noticed just now, when I said "thanks," that there was a brief commotion in the room. It goes without saying that I express my gratitude to every Member of Parliament and for every contribution on the agenda here. This also applies to Mr. Van Baarle. In my opening statement, I wanted to make it clear that, as far as I am concerned, it starts with the hit-and-run actions that took place in Amsterdam and with what is actually being carried out. From there, I subsequently reason that it concerns a group performing those actions — not another group, but the group performing those actions — and that it can be observed there that it involves young people, often boys, with a migration background. We must therefore take a very tough stance against them. In my view, therefore, there is a genuine integration problem here, which has ultimately manifested itself in this way, in part, over the past several decades.

I would like to add very emphatically that, regarding the latter—that black travel advisory—I am thinking of countries like Iran and North Korea. I am not thinking of people traveling back and forth to a red travel zone for a family visit, for example. I am thinking specifically of countries where we run the risk that Dutch citizens will subsequently be used as commodities—I am referring to hostage politics here—such as North Korea or Iran. The second motion. The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that the concept of a European Security Council has previously been discussed in various NATO and EU countries; considering that the security of Europe is coming under further pressure as a result of major threats and far-reaching geopolitical shifts, and that European dependence on the US for security, in particular, makes us vulnerable; considering that a European Security Council can be complementary to the structures of NATO and the EU and can also involve important non-EU countries such as Norway and the United Kingdom in important European security issues; requests the Government to explore the possibilities for a European Security Council within a European context, to submit concrete proposals to this effect, and to report to the House on the progress in Q3, quarter three, of 2025, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I think we do not differ much in that regard. I view ministerial responsibility differently from what I have heard today from previous speakers. To me, "ministerial responsibility" means being held accountable for what your organization does and for the evidently demonstrated errors the committee writes about. As a minister, you are then responsible, both now and for what happened in the past. Political accountability will have to be provided for that.

Political leadership—which you expect of a Prime Minister, and rightly so—entails demonstrating it both within and outside that political leadership. I demonstrate it externally, here in the House and elsewhere. You can also hold me accountable at any time for how cabinet policy manifests itself externally regarding all the points we have discussed and which are so essential to our constitutional state. Internally, it is up to the Cabinet. I will normally not speak here in this House about what I do internally. That is political leadership within the Cabinet, but you can hold me accountable for what I do externally when it comes to the unity of cabinet policy.

I have the feeling that nowadays we are dealing with that public-private interface a bit more relaxed. I also see that encouraging practorates provides more opportunities for this. I was able to choose that cut to the RIF because I see that precisely in the interim period… The RIF was actually always intended as a catalyst, as a starting point to enable collaborations. Subsequently, they were supposed to be consolidated within the partnership that is formed. But I also see that in the meantime we have other programs with the same goals. Particularly from the Growth Fund programs, these have emerged in recent years, such as the LLO-Katalysator. I already mentioned the scaling up of PPPs, quite deliberately, and also the more recent collaboration between the business community and educational institutions in the Beethoven Program, for which 450 million is available. In this context, we want to train MBO, HBO, and WO students in the high-tech sector precisely at that public-private interface. So we are doing a lot in the meantime and are dealing with that interface more relaxed. These programs will continue for the coming years and will also contribute to the goal that Mr. Soepboer and I hold so dear.

I just indicated to Mr Soepboer that I fully understand the House's wish to be more physically present in schools. Next, I look at the statutory duties the Inspectorate currently has: administrative supervision, system supervision, and supervision of schools. The moment you want to increase one of those duties, you have to consider how that relates to the other statutory duties. I see it as my task to enter into dialogue with the Inspectorate about this and to ensure that we do what is necessary to achieve that.

Let me add a very brief point, as Ms. Heite raised a few additional points. In any case, I can state how we, as Defence, are working on this, apart from this law. The first point was the translation into SMART objectives. For us, important guidelines for strengthening the armed forces at this moment are the requirements set by NATO and the shortcomings we have regarding what NATO asks of us. We have a greater focus on the defence of our alliance treaty territory. In that sense, what NATO asks is guiding. But it is not only that, for we also have tasks to fulfill beyond what NATO asks of us. During the budget debate, I discussed with Mr. Nordkamp, among other things, our obligations in the Caribbean, which are of course also of great importance. In addition, there are many other matters for which we do indeed set specific goals. A major problem for the armed forces, for example, is ensuring that we have filled all vacancies. We all have objectives regarding recruitment, the number of people we wish to attract through the service year, or reservists. I think we are trying to translate the major challenge before us—namely, keeping our country safe—into a great many specific objectives. That being said, we shouldn't make it too mathematical. After all, we have an ever-changing threat environment to which we must relate. You don't want to create a kind of paper reality that makes you appear very safe, but in which countries like Russia, Iran, and China operate in practice, while you are actually dealing more with the paper reality than with the actual threat landscape. Given all the objectives NATO asks of us and the targets we set, I think we are currently striking a good balance in this. Infrastructure is indeed a problem. We, as Defence, contribute to specific infrastructure because it is truly geared towards Defence. On the other hand: even with 2%, or if you were to spend even more than 2%, we do not have sufficient budget to adapt all bridges, all railway tracks, all roads, and that sort of thing in such a way that heavy equipment can travel over them in large numbers. That is part of the broader resilience approach, for which we, as the Cabinet, gave the go-ahead last week. In doing so, we will also look at these kinds of matters, and at how we can make infrastructure investments in such a way that they are also suitable for the large movements of equipment that we may face in the future as a transit country. Finally, I fully agreed that we must ensure that we, as European countries, are less dependent on third countries, and that we, as European countries, must be capable of producing more weapons and military equipment ourselves. Last week, we discussed at length that we have various European programs for this and that we are also doing a great deal about it at the national level. It should be clear that this is a very high priority.

A valid question from Mr. Grinwis. The reason I just listed that set of preconditions in such detail is because I consider them important preconditions within which you, as a company or as an industry, must have the opportunity to become more sustainable, whether it concerns a place on the power grid or the possibility of CCS to store your CO2. These are all factors that enable you as a company or as an industrial sector to do what we have agreed upon together. That is how I view what we can arrange and how we can better put those preconditions in order. Let me put it this way: do we then also meet what we agreed upon when we introduced that CO2 levy together? Ms. Postma asked: how do you deal with "you want to, but you can't"? We have already exchanged views on the hardship clause many times. There are variations on this, such as the carry-back or trading in exemption rights, and the trading platform, as mechanisms and possibilities to create some breathing room in the case of "you want to, but you can't". The CO2 levy is not intended to raise money, but truly as an incentive to become more sustainable. Where we are now seeing the revenue, partly through the development of price trajectories, the agreement is that the money returns to the Climate Fund so that it can be used again to make the industry more sustainable.

The motion regarding document no. 92 is by Ms. Dobbe and concerns a supplementary sanctions package and its broadening. We are currently working on the fifteenth sanctions package. I strongly advocated for this yesterday in the Foreign Affairs Council in Brussels. I agree with Ms. Dobbe that a robust package is needed. We are working hard on this. The Netherlands has a significant input in this regard. In any case, I am trying to ensure that at least part of that fifteenth sanctions package can be finalized before Christmas. I am pleased to submit the House's judgment on this motion. I am pleased to submit the House's judgment on the motion regarding document no. 93 by Mr. Van der Burg concerning a structural cooperation platform, with the following explanation. We are already bringing enforcement officers and the business community together. A sanctions conference will be held in January. You will receive a letter regarding this. In that regard, I view this motion as an encouragement. I do not intend to establish new institutions. I do, however, intend to bring people together, including the business community. We would also like to involve them in the shaping of the new sanctions law. Therefore, the motion regarding document no. 93 receives the House's judgment. The motion regarding document no. 94 by Mr. Van der Burg and others concerns the involvement of intelligence services. Cooperation with the intelligence services in this regard has recently been intensified. There are areas of overlap. Within existing frameworks, I wish to utilize these to the fullest extent regarding their work, for example concerning compliance or measures to prevent circumvention. Naturally, the services have their own working methods and reporting channels. We will maintain those lines, but I view the involvement of these intelligence services with optimism. Therefore: House's judgment. Mr. Chairman. Then there were a few questions. Mr. Paternotte asked about the approach to the Russian shadow fleet. We are making a strong effort to combat it, both through national enforcement and additional European measures. I also spoke about this again yesterday in the Foreign Affairs Council in Brussels, literally regarding the shadow fleet. We are in close contact with international partners, such as the US, the UK, and Ukraine, to strengthen European sanctions as effectively as possible in general. I would like to see additional measures this year. Very positive. All possibilities are also being utilized to tackle the shadow fleet, such as diplomatic outreach to the flag states. Mr Boswijk also asked about the shadow fleet and the listing of more ships. So we are working hard on that. He also spoke about lowering the oil price ceiling. That is determined within the G7 framework. That is again a subject we would like to discuss further with G7 countries. Incidentally, I will return to the shadow fleet later. Recently, as you may have seen, a meeting at a high official level took place in Estonia with a dozen member states of the European Union, in order to take this further.

Now I turn to the motions, Chair. The first motion, on document no. 4034, is from Mr. Dassen. It bears a strong resemblance to the fourth motion, the motion on document no. 4037 from Ms. Van der Werf and Mr. Paternotte. I advise against the first, and I leave the House's judgment on the fourth. This has to do with the wording. Ms. Van der Werf's motion refers to "an intergovernmental organization." That is indeed discussed. There are also various elements regarding how to specifically structure that. Mr. Dassen's motion works very specifically towards a European defence mechanism and playing a leading role in its specific setup, as articulated in the Bruegel report. It is not the case that we necessarily want to adopt all those elements from that report verbatim. We therefore wish to retain some room and flexibility to be able to discuss this further with the other European countries. The Minister of Finance is doing this in particular. That is why we feel comfortable with the motion regarding document no. 4037 and express that judgment to the House, but not with the motion regarding document no. 4034, which we must therefore advise against.

That is correct; in my opinion, we must also express appreciation for the people who work for the State. They are motivated individuals who truly do their best to make the Netherlands a little better every day. That is why I also said that it should not become a cold-hearted restructuring in which we go through the ministries with a blunt axe every other day. There must be an underlying vision for how that government can become smaller, but also stronger, in the future. I have made a number of suggestions in that regard. But I agree with Mr. Flach: if we collectively submit more than 5,000 motions in a year, then it indeed requires a certain self-discipline to prevent having to hire more and more policy officials to implement all those motions. So it lies in that area, but in my view, it also lies in the extent to which we, as the House, will co-govern on details. We have had a tendency to do that in recent years, so my party group will certainly take that into account in how we approach debates. Let us try to keep each other sharp on that, and the colleagues as well, is what I would like to say to Mr. Flach.

That discussion has indeed not been concluded. Mr. Dassen puts it very well. There were a number of countries, including the United Kingdom and France, that have previously expressed their views on this publicly. They said that they were willing to consider it or develop initiatives for it. The Netherlands has always stated—and this has also been shared in previous parliamentary debates—that we want to participate in those discussions on the matter and that, in principle, we want to contribute constructively to the discussion, but that has indeed not yet led to a training program in Ukraine, carried out by various European countries. This is also because it is not one of Ukraine's major priorities. If you ask them if they want us to train in Ukraine, they naturally say yes, but when the Ukrainians call for support, this is not typically in the top five of things they consider important. You can actually see now that the entire discussion on security guarantees, which has gained momentum since last week—how are we going to shape these, with or without a European presence in Ukraine?—is cutting across the discussion on training. We will have to see how this develops further in the coming period. I think that the priorities and attention are now more focused on shaping those robust security guarantees. Part of that could potentially involve training taking place in Ukraine, but that will need to be brought together. I think that answers the questions regarding Ukraine. Mr. Eerdmans also had a question about the robust security guarantees, but the Prime Minister has previously stated that it is still too early to say anything concrete about that. It still needs to be further elaborated and considered what the role of Ukraine, European countries, the United States, and any third countries participating in such a case will be. It is too early to say anything concrete about that at this moment. Then there was a question from Ms. Yeşilgöz. I have now reached the section on NATO and our own armed forces. Ms. Yeşilgöz asked what is needed in the coming years to keep us safe, including within NATO. For NATO, the starting point is always: what threats are coming our way? They then translate that into the associated risks and what needs to be done to reduce those security risks. Military capabilities are required for this. Over the past few months, we have entered into discussions with NATO regarding the total capabilities NATO needs to defend our alliance and the contribution the Netherlands must make to this. We have now made further progress in the discussion on this matter, in that dialogue. We are currently translating this into what it means financially for us if we were to give substance to it. I previously promised the House that we would communicate this to the House during the first quarter—which is therefore already in the short term. We can then indicate somewhat more concretely what order of magnitude is required if we wish to make our contribution. However, there is, of course, a longer timeframe to give further substance to this.

Opinions may be divided on this, but yesterday morning at 9:15 a.m. I gave my statement. That was, once again, a statement regarding support for the mayor, and also for the mayor of Amsterdam. Once again, I have also been clear about the right to demonstrate. I really try in various ways to avoid responding to every individual message, because that brings with it a dynamic of its own. I constantly weigh that up. That is why I choose my words with extraordinary care when expressing support. I primarily express support. Incidentally, that is also how I did it last night in an otherwise exceptionally enjoyable debate in the Senate regarding the General Political Debates, in response to questions from the parliamentary group leader of the Labour Party (GroenLinks).

Chair, thank you. I just noticed that I am seeing the Minister more often and for longer this week than my own husband, I believe. But perhaps he is watching from home. Chair. Today we are discussing the Act on the Implementation of the EU Directive on Adequate Minimum Wages. We are positive about the implementation of the quantitative criteria and the indicative reference values, whereby these will also become part of the four-yearly evaluation of the minimum wage. However, we do have a number of questions for the Minister. First of all, we ask the Minister how these criteria and reference values relate to the broad consideration made every four years during the evaluation of the minimum wage. Can the Minister provide an outline of what current evaluation practice looks like at this moment? Then there is the indicative reference value stating that the net disposable income of a full-time minimum wage earner amounts to, on average, 128% of the necessary costs of living. In this system, disposable income includes not only income from employment, but also central government schemes and municipal schemes, such as allowances; we have already heard a lot about that this evening. While we understand that this system accurately reflects the practical situation, we do ask the Minister how desirable he considers it to classify schemes such as allowances under disposable income. Can he further explain why this specific system has been chosen? Mr. Chairman. Then, regarding the indicative reference value that the gross statutory minimum wage amounts to 50% of the median wage. We support the use of this reference value, as it accurately reflects how the minimum wage relates to wages higher up in the wage distribution in the Netherlands. We understand that 50% was chosen, particularly in light of the most recently known figure, the 49.14% calculated by the OECD for the Netherlands. Here, too, we wish to emphasize that determining the level of this reference value is a national competence. However, we ask the Minister whether he can outline the considerations made in choosing that 50% as an indicative reference value. Why was another value not chosen, for example 60%, which we have discussed frequently here this evening? Mr. Chairman. Now, regarding the youth minimum wage. As my party group pointed out during the General Political Debates, the economic security of young people is also of great importance. Besides Ireland, we are the only EU country with a general youth minimum wage. The Minister is currently conducting an exploration regarding the desired level of the youth minimum wage. We await the results of this exploration with interest. The New Social Contract considers it of great importance that young people do not fall below the social minimum. Can the Minister indicate how he intends to prevent this? Chair. Finally, regarding the collective labour agreement coverage ratio. This has been fixed at 71.8% for 22%. Because this is below 80%, an action plan must be drawn up in accordance with the directive. We would like to hear from the Minister what the intended process is for drawing up this action plan. In particular, we would also like to hear how the Minister intends to inform the House about the drafting of the action plan during the process. That concludes my remarks. Thank you.

That is a view of humanity that has undoubtedly taken shape over 67 years. But it is not merely a personal choice. It is also a choice made by the Cabinet: what do we want to stand for together? That, I think, is also what the voter is asking for. The voter is not so much asking about my view of humanity, but about what this Cabinet stands for. The Cabinet stands for: solving real problems—problems that we experience today as major problems—focusing on them, putting energy into them, and getting to work on them. In this regard, I would like to say the following to Mr. Stassen. I have worked in public service for 40 years. If you work in public service for 40 years and put your heart and soul into it, you do so because you want to make a difference for people. Otherwise, you would not work for the government for so long in the type of positions I have held.

Over the past few minutes, Ms. Yeşilgöz has been giving me and other colleagues feedback in her own unique way. That is permissible, but I do not think it strengthens the debate. I am trying to lay out here, in all calmness, how this Cabinet can strongly stand up for a safe Netherlands and a safe Europe. And I note, in all calmness, that the Cabinet is not taking a position in the negotiations in advance. It is very simple: whoever does not take a position is at the mercy of the waves.

Responsibility comes in many forms here. I believe the challenge now, for all of us but certainly also for us as those responsible for the system, is to articulate more clearly within the current system as we know it who exactly holds which responsibility. Where possible, and where responsibility is currently distributed—which I tried to phrase in my introduction as "a responsibility that differs, where the various organizations also assume that the other will take the responsibility"—I think we can refine this in the short term. In this way, we ensure that we are very clear with the sector: just for the record, the child protection organization has control over, and therefore responsibility for, the child protection measures. Even if that responsibility lies partly with others, it remains a unique, individual responsibility of the child protector. That is an example of where we can clarify exactly where which responsibility lies, to prevent the fragmentation inherent in the system from leading to diffuse responsibility. The pain lies in the diffuse nature. Another aspect is, of course, the long term: the reallocation of responsibilities. But that is part of the future scenario.

I strongly object to that. I do not consider it correct that Ms. Maatoug links the discussion regarding whether or not one is better off to one specific measure every time. Ms. Maatoug should also be honest and state that we are explicitly alleviating the burden on workers by introducing an extra tax bracket and by taking numerous measures; with this, we are also trying—and hopefully colleague Keijzer will address this shortly—to finally reduce that marginal tax rate. I have honestly admitted that this is not succeeding for the child budget. But regarding the housing allowance, we are actually taking a step that will make working, and working more hours, more rewarding. We are not finished with that task. With regard to limiting the marginal tax rate and the question of whether one is actually better off if one starts working while receiving benefits or if one works more hours, I truly see a major challenge regarding the reform of income support and the design of the benefits and tax systems. I also sincerely hope that we will have a serious discussion with this House.

Chair. With the exception of a single remark regarding the proposed amendment by Member Deen, I will limit myself to the motions. In view of the debate in the first round, I would, however, like to offer a few perspectives from the point of view of legislative quality. This therefore concerns the amendment by Member Deen. First of all, I note that it is not preferable to use budget laws to implement changes to other laws. The Council of State has also noted this in various matters. In this specific example, the added complexity arises that the law proposed to be suspended requires a qualified majority for one person for one year under the Constitution. This raises questions regarding the effect of the amendment and the consequences of the amendment for the handling of votes. The House of Representatives and the Senate themselves bear a responsibility regarding the admissibility of such an amendment in voting procedures. That goes without saying. I cannot fully assess the situation at this moment, but there is indeed a constitutional lower limit. An amendment that actually has consequences for the level of the allowance of one of the members entitled to the allowance shall not take effect unless the bill of which the amendment will form part is adopted by a majority of at least two-thirds in both Houses, as prescribed by the Constitution. Mr. Chairman. I will now turn to the motions. I advise against the motion on document No. 6 concerning a proposal for the implementation of a ceremonial monarchy. The letter does not concern a ceremonial monarchy. I believe Mr. Aartsen also made a remark about that. The motion on document No. 7 requests a substantial improvement in public accountability. I advise against this motion, because in an earlier opinion issued in 2023, the Council of State, after much consultation and discussion, concluded that there is no longer any room within the constitutional framework. The motion on document No. 6 concerns the structural financing of the CTIVD. I request that this motion be postponed until I have been able to hold discussions with the Ministers of Justice and Security, the Interior, and Finance.

Chair, thank you. Thanks to Ms. Pijpelink for these questions. I have indeed read the figures as well, and not only that; we also monitor what is happening regarding external hiring. And I do not consider this a positive development. In that regard, Ms. Pijpelink has me on her side. It is primarily important that students have a familiar face in the classroom, a good permanent teacher. At the same time, it is also important that public money we invest in education lands in education and not in the pockets of commercial agencies. "What am I doing to counter this?" was Ms. Pijpelink's question. That is, at the very least, conveying that message very strongly to employers in the education sector. I subsequently support this with a legislative proposal currently in the works regarding strategic HRM policy (SHRM), which ensures that good employership actually goes hand in hand with good personnel policy by school boards. In this, we are establishing legal requirements for permanent and larger contracts, among other things, because I know through my contacts in the education field that one of the reasons why teachers who only have small, permanent contracts ultimately opt for a self-employed position, for example, is that they feel they are being strung along. I find that unacceptable, especially in these times of shortages. You must retain good people through permanent and larger contracts. We are going to enshrine this in law. This is also in line with the Kwint/Westerveld motion, of which Ms. Pijpelink is undoubtedly aware. I expect to send the bill to the Council of State in early 2025.

I also watched the debate between Mr. Paternotte and Mr. Van Campen with pleasure. I saw a sort of struggle among the liberals in the European group regarding the question: who actually wants to be the best in Europe? But given the entire political landscape, I am concerned about the pro-European coalition falling apart, even though I think those differences are not all that great. The reason I am very critical of immediately saying "we must abandon Schengen" is that it opens up a whole discussion about whether Schengen should end altogether or whether we should move towards a mini-system. I do not rule out that we will end up there if Hungary really persists, but there are still many other issues on the escalation ladder that we could deploy. I hope that we can find common ground here and say: if necessary and we reach that point, then we will support this cabinet to help ensure that Hungary is kicked out. But let us take a few other steps before we get there. I have the feeling that we agree on that and that we will find common ground, rather than making a huge difference now, as seemed to be happening for a moment in the little debate just now.

Chair. I think I am going slightly over my allotted time, as I wish to offer a brief reflection on the debate. I am not yet reassured about the role we all can and will fulfill within the rule of law. I wish to offer this as a critical note, but I want to end on a positive note. I think it is commendable that we, as the House, are seeking solutions and wish to strengthen democracy. It is therefore good that a proposal is being put forward for an adjustment to the electoral system. I am moderately enthusiastic about colleague Erkens’ proposal, as I indicated yesterday, but it is an innovation. In this regard, I would like to draw attention to the following. It also seems fitting to me to hold a debate on the initiative submitted by colleagues Bikker and Dassen regarding the expansion of the House and the inclusion of a long-term vision. I am casting a sideways glance at the Minister. There has been no appreciation or response from the Cabinet regarding this yet. It seems very fitting to me to combine these matters, as this would demonstrate to everyone that we wish to strengthen democracy. Thank you, Chair.

I have previously stated that I hope and expect that municipalities in the Netherlands will give their full cooperation to the Minister of Asylum and Migration to provide all assistance during the coming summer and in the period thereafter, and to ensure, on a voluntary basis, that as long as the influx does not decrease, those coming to this country receive decent accommodation in asylum reception centers. I truly count on the municipalities to take these steps with us, precisely out of that responsibility for good governance which we must safeguard between the central government and the local authorities.

If at any point the air is truly out of it, I will be the first to admit it. That way, we are being as realistic as possible. However, the trend is that over the past ten years—with the exception of one year—we have all had substantial deviations from the budget. I think the Minister agrees with me that this does not do justice to the political discussion regarding the allocation of those resources. You would have preferred to have had that discussion sooner. That is one aspect. On the other hand, it also affects the sense of financial reliability, because you often present a large surplus almost simultaneously with concerns about yet another new budget. If you do that year after year, it also touches upon the reliability of the information we share with each other. If we can zoom out from the technical aspects for a moment and agree to work towards the smallest possible difference between the final outcome and the estimate, then I would be happy to cooperate. I sense that it is also the Minister's goal to prevent the resources from being—as it sounded briefly in a parenthetical remark—simply hoarded by portfolio ministers and underspending from going unreported. By doing so, you also deny the House the political leeway to make adjustments over the course of the year.

Over the past two and a half months, I have stated with some regularity that I am not going to judge the House or its Members. I stand by that. I have stated what I stand for as Prime Minister, and I stand for everyone. I have explained, just slightly more extensively than usual, how I view societal structures, how I view people, and how I believe people should actually treat one another. That seems perfectly clear to me. That means I stand for that. I grant everyone in this House the full freedom to say and do whatever they must, as long as it is done within the rules of the law.

I pledge "undiminished" without reservation, for that word is a standard part of my vocabulary. We are doing whatever is necessary. With regard to the multi-year contracts and the sustainability of the investments, I can state that the defense budgets of many countries are increasing enormously. These also help to ensure that the defense industry can explicitly rely on them. I gladly assure Mr. Klaver that the Cabinet will make available the amounts required at that time. During next year's spring budget, we will undoubtedly reconsider how we can disburse the next tranches together. This will be done with the understanding—you might say that those words mean little to you and offer little certainty—that this Cabinet provides full political, military, and financial support to Ukraine. Our support remains undiminished.

Chair, I will do my best. There are 26 motions, so it took some work. I had one question outstanding, that of Mr. Dassen regarding the European Sky Shield. My apologies. This caused a moment of confusion, at least on my part. There is, in fact, also a European Sky Shield Initiative in which 23 European member states participate, but I understand that Mr. Dassen was referring to the Sky Shield proposal drafted by a group of military experts and former Members of Parliament, and which was also signed by Mr. Dassen. This is an important initiative aimed at keeping Ukraine's airspace safe. Ways to keep the airspace safe are naturally part of the current discussions being held by the military planners of the coalition of the willing. The valuable ideas from the proposal to which Mr. Dassen refers are naturally being included in those discussions. Chair, I will now turn to the motions. The motion on document no. 2217 by Mr. Dassen: advise against. NATO is currently mapping out which military capabilities are required. Europe must do more itself, but NATO remains the cornerstone of European security. The motion on document no. 2218 regarding the promotion of European intelligence capacity: judgment of the House, with the explanation that we will advocate for national intelligence services to contribute to the EU's intelligence position in cooperation, with a line under national intelligence services. Then the motion on document no. 2219 regarding the action plan for talent from the US, also by Mr. Dassen: advised against. There is no proposal for an approach specifically aimed at the US, but I do promise Mr. Dassen that we will look into how we can extend the program to the US as well. The motion on document no. 2220 by Mr. Timmermans: advised against. This refers specifically to "not putting it up for discussion". After all, we must always be critical and be able to put proposals up for discussion. See also the letter from the Cabinet of last Friday. Then the motion on document no. 2221 by Mr. Timmermans regarding supporting joining the Czech initiative Radio Free Europe: judgment of the House. Earlier today, Minister Veldkamp signed the technical declaration, so we are busy with that.

In the declaration on the rule of law, the parties forming the government agreed that we may hold an open, fierce, and honest debate with one another. I stand for that; that is part of a democracy. We also agreed to keep each other safe and to work towards a positive administrative climate. Does all of this fit in with that? I view that question as a rhetorical one. Look, if a Member of Parliament makes this appeal to me here, then that Member of Parliament is free to do so. I am in charge of my own words and my own powers. And I am stating here that not for a moment did I consider using the power granted to me by law to dismiss the Mayor of Amsterdam. I am saying this today in your Chamber, but I also said it immediately outside yesterday, to the media, after the first term in your Chamber, because I felt it was very important that mayors in this country know that this Minister of the Interior stands for all mayors in the Netherlands. I stand for it and I stand behind it, and that is why I also felt it was important that there was no longer any ambiguity regarding this. I believe I also started this contribution today by immediately setting out my position here crystal clear. The Prime Minister did the same yesterday in clear terms.
If I express the unity of cabinet policy in the words I choose, then the cabinet stands for what it stands for.

Thanks to Mr. Omtzigt. It should be clear that, acting in the spirit of the unity of cabinet policy, cabinet members essentially always undertake activities as cabinet members. That will be the line we agree upon together, or rather, the line we just discussed once again. At various times, tweets are simply not sent for procedural reasons, in order to explicitly safeguard the unity of cabinet policy together. Many ministers are new. I think it is very important to realize that being a government minister is different from being a Member of Parliament, that being a government minister is different from whatever you have done, and that being a government minister is also different from being a civil servant. That entails different responsibilities. We will undoubtedly hold that debate together this coming Friday as well. I am convinced that this will lead to the conclusion that we place the unity of cabinet policy above any other conceivable line.

My answer to that, then, is that as far as I am concerned, supervision must be organized as effectively as possible. The moment you say "go visit all schools" and that becomes a sort of checklist to visit all schools at a certain pace within a certain number of years, the question is whether school visits equate to effective supervision. The Inspectorate indicates "we have that mix, based also on the statutory duties the Inspectorate has" — I was referring to the statutory duty of four-yearly administrative supervision based on the private member's bill, for example — but also those other investigations, the combination of investigations that the Inspectorate conducts. If that ensures you have the right overview upon which to act, that is more important to me than saying "you are going to visit all those schools by definition," because you want to be where the risks are greatest, where you run the greatest risk that quality is not up to par, where schools need help, and where students receive the right education.

In my opinion, compared to what NATO is asking of us, we are currently facing a real shortage, even with all the investments we are making. NATO is therefore asking individual countries for capacity to contribute to our collective defense. We will really have to work hard to come anywhere near what NATO is asking of us. In my view, it would be a great waste if we were to spend our scarce capacity and precious energy now on setting up a parallel EU structure in case the United States is unwilling to make an effort. In my opinion, the call to European countries from the Trump administration and the United States is primarily to do more to defend their own continent: deploy more capabilities, ensure that you deploy more personnel, and ensure that certain strategic tasks are taken over. I was just discussing the latter with Mr. Boswijk. In my view, all our attention should be focused on that, and we should not create a parallel structure alongside NATO within the EU. I also say to Ms. Van der Werf: be careful what you wish for. For a very long time, it has cost the EU an enormous amount of effort to release a small amount of money that we want to allocate to Ukraine within a European context, simply because Hungary is obstructing it. I am pleased with the NATO structure and with the weight of some non-EU countries, which ensure that 32 countries are ready to defend us the moment things get really tense. However, when I compare this to the EU's decision-making structures, I become even more worried, rather than less so.

I have emphasized investigative capacity a few times. Unfortunately, we are also lacking some personnel in this area to conduct investigations as effectively as possible. I have had the opportunity to hold many discussions recently, including with the police. They have explicitly indicated that, on the one hand, more training places are needed in the short term at the Police Academy for new lateral-entry detectives. However, for the long term, there is also a need for modular detective education, so that it becomes possible to receive full training. To this end, I have prepared another motion, with the explicit request and hope that this Minister will enter into discussions to see if such a separate detective training program is indeed feasible. The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that there is a great practical need for increased investigative capacity for criminal investigation; noting that the number of training places at the Police Academy for new, lateral-entry detectives is limited; noting that in the long term there is a need for modular detective education, offering both the possibility of receiving full training and the possibility for uniformed personnel to transfer; requests the government to explore, together with the police leadership, how a separate, more targeted modular detective training program can be realized, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I refer back to my answer to Ms. Lahlah. This falls into the category of questions such as: does legislation need to change to prevent these excesses? The idea is not to target or restrict the majority of demonstrations, but to prevent these incidents. That is part of the question posed to the WODC. For example, we also asked the WODC to examine how this is regulated in other countries. In England, the Public Order Act was passed in 2023. It imposes heavy penalties for occupying infrastructure, destroying it, or chaining oneself to it. That law has been in force for a year and a half now, so there is case law regarding it by now. Work has been done on a similar law in Italy. We are incorporating that into the WODC's research.

In the Netherlands, we have a neutral civil service. Ministers do not interfere substantively with the appointment of top civil servants. The achievement of the Dutch system is that the civil service apparatus therefore does not change when the political composition of the cabinet changes. That is a guarantee for the quality of that apparatus, so to speak. That quality is high. I do not say this merely from my own experience; it is also evident time and again from international comparative research. I trust in it, I count on it, and I also know that this cabinet, every minister sitting here in this section K, can rely on excellent civil service support and on an excellent civil service execution of whatever is politically decided here.

Quite right. I fully understand that. I have just criticized Mr. Paternotte, and through him actually several of my colleagues, for the fact that during this Middle East debate we are focusing solely on Israel. I do understand it. I also see that within the discussion on Israel, we are mainly talking about Gaza, whereas the West Bank should also play a role. We are seeing developments there as well, about which I have questions for the Minister, which I immediately posed during this interruption, such as: how does the Minister view this? We must not forget that there must always be a balance between the following questions. How do we put pressure on the countries that can put pressure on Hamas? How do we ensure that, whether via Qatar or otherwise, Hamas is pressured to release the hostages, to cease hostilities, or to withdraw from Gaza? And on the other hand: how can the Israeli government be pressured to adhere to the laws of war and to provide humanitarian aid? I realize that to some, it may come across as if the VVD is more critical of Israel than of an organization like Hamas. I have absolutely no confidence whatsoever in Hamas, but it must continue to be stated that that organization needs to be put under pressure just as much. However, I am also going to ask the Minister shortly to look into what further instruments he has to make clear to the Netanyahu government that they must adhere to the laws of war and that emergency aid must be provided. At the same time—and I say this to the CDA as well, and others involved—we, as the House and as the Netherlands, must not overestimate our position. That is also what the Minister has said continuously. We see that if we make very strong, harsh statements or decisions, what we want does not automatically happen. In that regard, I say: I prefer effective measures, or the attempt to achieve effective measures, rather than tough words. Finally, I immediately add that such effectiveness has so far been barely evident in either variant.

I also give the House a judgment on the motion regarding document no. 151 by Mr. Flach. Now, the motion regarding document no. 152 by Mr. Vermeer concerning the investigations: that I may not start new investigations that delay the implementation of nuclear energy. I have started a number of investigations in the past period which, as Ms. Kröger already stated, are legally required and are part of the project procedure. In a few cases, these do have an effect on the lead time, but I must conduct them, also in the context of being able to make a solid and sound decision later on. Therefore, I advise against this motion. Then the motion regarding document no. 153 by Ms. Koekkoek. We can explore those possibilities. We will do so, so with that: House a judgment. And then the fifteenth motion, regarding document no. 154, by Mr. Erkens and later added by Messrs. Bontenbal and Vermeer. I also give the House a judgment on that motion. Chair. Then I have a question from Ms. Kröger regarding the withdrawal of KHNP. The South Korean company KHNP has informed the Netherlands that it will not play a role as a technology supplier. This decision follows earlier decisions by KHNP to withdraw from nuclear power plant construction in other European countries as well, such as Sweden and Slovenia. Naturally, I find it regrettable that the company has made this decision. We have collaborated closely, including on the technical feasibility study and the third-party review. However, KHNP's decision changes nothing regarding the way in which the Netherlands allows companies to compete for the construction of nuclear power plants. Preparations will continue as planned with the other two suppliers. KHNP's withdrawal is a strategic choice made by the company itself and follows, as stated, earlier decisions in Sweden and Slovenia. Chair. Ms. Kröger also asked me about the situation in France and about a study or report that was recently published there. I will inquire with my colleague in France about the content of the report and also about EDF's international ambitions. I will report on those contacts in the letter containing the next update on nuclear energy, which I promised for early May. Finally, Ms. Kröger asked me a question about Westinghouse and the US, and whether we should really want to cooperate with them. Westinghouse is a private company in Canadian hands. We cooperate intensively with the Americans in the field of nuclear energy. America is and remains an important partner for the Netherlands. Political weighting is not part of the selection process.

Let me begin by saying that the Education Supervision Act stipulates that the intensity of supervision must be linked to the quality of education and compliance with the law. The risk-based component is therefore legally established. This is also one of the OECD principles. The other characteristics of the manner in which the Inspectorate conducts its supervision are also regularly reviewed via peer review. I understand Mr. Soepboer's wish regarding this point. A peer review was also recently initiated in which the Inspectorate's working methods are once again being independently assessed. If we come to discuss this, I hope to be able to provide Mr. Soepboer with more reassurance regarding the substantiation.

I understand the background to Ms. Bikker's question, but we have the Ukraine debate today. Regarding all discussions concerning the legitimate wishes regarding priorities, burdens, and support for people who actually find themselves in less fortunate circumstances than many of us, as we sit here, I also said last time that we will hold those during the Spring Budget. I said the same thing regarding the Defence point. We are simply going to do that during the Spring Budget. We will then have that discussion back here in the House, regarding the weighing of everything that ultimately needs to happen in financial terms.

I have already said a few times that I really try to refrain from responding to individual statements made by party leaders via social media. At the same time, yesterday I spoke out clearly twice about the support the Mayor of Amsterdam deserves. I did so yesterday morning and last night, and I am doing so here today. I believe that is the right place, and I believe it is my duty; let me be clear about that as well. In doing so, I also expressed support for the fact that the right to demonstrate applied fully on October 7, regardless of my opinion regarding that demonstration. In my opinion, I can hardly be clearer in a normative sense, unless you ask me to explicitly review an individual statement by a party leader. I have already said several times that I really do not intend to do that. No matter how often you ask me to do so in your House, I will not do it because, firstly, I believe it is the freedom of a party leader to express an opinion on a matter. That applies to individual Members of Parliament as well. Secondly, the debate on this matter really must take place in this House, including among you. That debate can also take place with the Cabinet if it is on the agenda in a debate where it belongs. In this case, that is in a debate with the Ministry of the Interior.

I will now turn to the Defence Budget. With regard to the Defence Budget, I have stated on several occasions that Europe will have to assume greater responsibility for maintaining Europe's security. Investing extra in national defence also applies to the Netherlands. The priority in this regard is that all NATO allies meet—that is the first line—the agreed defence expenditure level of 2% of GDP as soon as possible. After all, there is still a substantial number of countries that do not meet the 2%. In the context of the NATO summit, it will be examined what is needed for Defence and how this will be covered. Naturally, this will be done in close consultation with your House, following an orderly decision-making process and with a plan, as Mr. Timmermans also requested. I will not anticipate this decision-making process at this time. You know that we will undoubtedly discuss the Defence Budget during the Spring Memorandum decision-making, also in light of the motions adopted here in the House on that point.

Chair. Two motions on my part. The House, having heard the debate, noting that the vast majority of labour migrants in the Netherlands originate from other EU Member States; whereas these Member States often experience problems as a result, because this causes skilled workers to leave their countries of origin; noting that, in addition, migrants from outside the EU are increasingly starting work in the Netherlands via another EU Member State through onward posting; whereas further enlargement of the European Union will lead to increased migration to the Netherlands, as previous enlargements have shown, while greater control over labour migration is desirable and necessary; whereas the State Commission on Demographic Developments 2050 has explicitly pointed out the negative consequences of, among other things, EU labour migration on our demographics, and recommends that the Dutch Government actively participate in the European debate on demographics and call attention to the relative exceptional position of our country in this regard; requests the government to enter into discussions at the European level regarding the possibilities of limiting labour migration within the European Union and to seek support for the possibility of allowing Member States the scope to introduce residence or work permits for migrant workers originating from another EU Member State or who have entered the EU via another Member State, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I already told Mr. Wilders that defense spending was discussed extensively during the debate last time. It is emphatically clear that the weighting of defense spending against other expenditures must be carried out. I understand the weighing of priorities and the associated decision-making process. Regarding the Ukraine funds, I naturally hear very well what you are saying. But I also say: we have an outline agreement and a coalition programme. We will have to look together at how we deal with this during the elaboration in the Spring Memorandum.

I have great respect for that. I myself, of course, had the privilege of serving in the House for several years, which was a great honor, by the way. Indeed, I know I am supported by a large room full of civil servants, but also by the ministries. People work incredibly hard, and there is an incredible amount of talent around. And indeed, as an MP, you are almost on your own. You have one policy officer. That person works incredibly hard too. I have tremendous respect for the work being done there. Perhaps we should also hold a debate on the nature of those relationships, in order to strengthen the position of the House as well. I am very much in favor of that. In my role as spokesperson for Finance, I have also often made proposals to strengthen the House's budgetary authority, because a strong parliament also leads to a strong democracy. Mr. Chairman. I am digressing. We are returning to the business succession scheme. That is a complex undertaking. In light of the debate on inheritance and gift tax, I would like to say the following. If you have a business that you wish to continue, you can pass it on to the next generation without incurring a substantial tax liability and essentially having to liquidate your company. That is also an advantage, because we have many family businesses in our country. These are wonderful companies that contribute to making this country prosperous. Family businesses also provide stability and often good employership; let us state that upfront. The image is often created that employers are all terrible people who squeeze everyone dry. The employers I speak with have an incredible heart for their business. When someone falls ill, tears often well up in those employers' eyes, because they often know the people involved, know the families behind them, and come together for dinner. In that sense, we have a very beautiful society, because citizens and businesses do not stand opposed to each other, but share the understanding that we must do this together and try to make the country more prosperous. That is my introduction to the BOR. For the BOR ensures the continuity of those wonderful companies that we have. However, there are a number of questions regarding it. After all, there are political parties that say: expand the Business Succession Relief (BOR). There are also political parties that say: restrict it. In the past, the previous cabinet also tried to strike a balance in this. My predecessor in the role of State Secretary, Mr. Van Rij, conducted the debate with the House on this matter from the previous cabinet. He said: let us, also in light of tackling tax regulations, limit the exemption somewhat, from 80% to around 70%. The House then said: no, that must go to approximately 75%. The condition was attached to that was that the threshold would go from 1 million to around 1.2 million, if I recall correctly. There were still two loose ends: the holding period and the continuation requirement. These are now being addressed with this Tax Plan. I am really digging deep into my memory, because this involves a lot of technical details. The holding period remains the same, but is extended by the state pension age to tackle tax structures such as 'walker' and 'carousel' Business Succession Schemes (BORs). The continuation requirement is extended from five to three years. That sounds strange intuitively, but it is an extension. That is what is before us. In that light, Ms. Maatoug asks why the Cabinet did not consider extending the holding period, to which I just referred, upon death. Those are simply political choices. That was not chosen. I do point out, however, that at a later age, at the state pension eligibility age, the holding period is tightened. For each year after the state pension age, the holding period is extended by six months, if I recall correctly. Let me take a closer look at that, because I am really digging deep into my memory right now. But in any case, that was done to tackle those carousel companies and 'walker' companies. Then Ms. Maatoug also asked what the then-government said in 1981 and whether there was a danger to the continuity of family businesses at the time. This is a fine question. She asked what the then-State Secretary—we are talking about 1981, so 43 years ago—said about the danger that inheritance tax would jeopardize continuity. I have forwarded this question to the National Archives. I cannot ascertain what was said at the time. I would gladly send that to you by letter, but I am also looking at Ms. Maatoug for a moment to see if we may consider this question rhetorical, so that civil servants at the National Archives do not have to start running around again to send this to you in writing. But I do understand the political point Ms. Maatoug wants to make. We have differing views on that. Chair. I will try to answer the next question more on the substance. Ms. Maatoug asked about the substantiation of the dilution scheme. We have discussed that often. Let me repeat what I said about that in this debate. I must add right away that we are really diving into the technical details here, but that is permissible in this debate. The Business Succession Relief (BOR) is intended for entrepreneurs in cases of genuine business succession. I have already mentioned that briefly. The dilution rule is an extension of that, for example in situations where the estate is distributed among multiple surviving relatives, i.e., when the business is transferred to multiple children. The dilution rule has existed since 2010. It was introduced to prevent diluted interests in an operating company from no longer qualifying for the BOR, and thus jeopardizing the continuity of a family business. That was the reason for the dilution rule. I estimate—my political intuition tells me—that Ms. Maatoug would like to tighten it. I do not think a majority for that can be found in this House, given the importance of the continuity of family businesses. In light of the BOR, Ms. Maatoug also asked why the Cabinet intends to assess the 5% at the level of the donor or the testator, not at the level of the recipient. That is also a very good, technical question. The honest answer is that this is how it has historically evolved. To assess whether the Business Succession Relief (BOR) can be applied, the focus has always been on the interest held by the donor or the testator, not on the extent of the recipient's interest. Sometimes legislation evolves historically. Mr. Vermeer asked why transfers to a brother-in-law are exempt from transfer tax, but transfers to spouses are not. He asked if I intend to extend that exemption to spouses and other direct family members. Essentially, Mr. Vermeer is asking for an extension. If he phrases the question in this way, it is an unfunded request. I must reject that. However, I do understand the background to his question, because this too has evolved historically and we live in different times. So let me promise that we will conduct further research into the reason for this, into the financial consequences of expanding it, and into how it might potentially be covered. I am not making a promise that we will arrange it. But at least then you know what we are talking about. After all, I actually understand very well why Mr. Vermeer is asking about this. I hereby make that commitment. Mr. Vermeer also inquired about the cabinet proposal for hybrid shares for the business sector and why, in his assessment, it would make business succession so complicated. In his assessment, it hinders rather than supports it. I often hear Mr. Vermeer speak about hybrid shares. He is therefore passionate about them. Let me state the following regarding this. Under the current and future regulations, preferred shares do not qualify for the Business Succession Relief (BOR), unless they have been issued in the context of a phased business succession. Hybrid shares are shares with characteristics of a preferred share and characteristics of an ordinary share. That is why they are hybrid. Under current law, the entire share is classified as either a "preferred share" or an "ordinary share". The law provides no guidance on how this should be determined further. Therefore, a definition of preferred shares has been included in the legislative proposal. It thus also regulates the extent to which hybrid shares qualify for the BOR. In our view, this provides clarity for practical application and prevents a situation where the entire share qualifies or does not qualify for the BOR, despite the fact that the share is partly preferred and partly not. That is the answer to that question. I hope to have been able to convince Mr. Vermeer of this proposal with this. Mr. Chairman, we are going to pick up the pace. I have reached the end of my answers. There are a few follow-up questions.

Chair, thank you. Without security, there is no freedom. Security is the core task of the government. The government must deliver on security. Security is not a static possession. Unfortunately, we see this time and again in the many examples. We have set out a strong ambition in the framework agreement. We have structurally allocated extra funds in the budget and subsequently made another 100 million available. I am satisfied with that. But there is always work to be done. Today I will discuss four themes. The first is undermining. Undermining is a catch-all term, but we are talking about organized drug crime. It is infiltrating our neighborhoods. Earlier this week I was in Southwest Brabant and I saw and heard with my own eyes again how mayors there have to work hard to keep the neighborhoods safe. We must continue with focus and tenacity with what was initiated earlier. We must tackle this multi-headed monster, as we often call it here. That is also necessary to address the concrete rot in our constitutional state — for that is what it leads to. As far as I am concerned, we should continue working according to the familiar three-pronged approach: prevention, repression, and breaking the financial revenue model. I have one question regarding all three. Prevention: we had a wonderful working visit regarding this in Amsterdam-Zuidoost. I spoke with many people who are working in the neighborhoods day in and day out, and who kept running into the fact that they would really like to be able to share more information with each other. I read in the written questions regarding this that this is in the hands of the Data Sharing Task Force. But what is the current status of that, I ask the Minister. Then repression. We must punish severely where necessary. Where is the increase in sentencing guidelines for drug offenses? When will we get that to the House? And finally, breaking the financial revenue model; tough stuff, but very much needed. Last year, I submitted a motion for that coordinating role regarding gatekeepers, partly in response to the research into this by KPMG. That was adopted in February 2024. Where is that approach? Then my second point for today: police capacity. First of all, I want to pay a huge compliment to the police. They always step forward where others step back; we have seen that again recently. I also think it is very good that we are going to discuss the approach to violence against officers in a separate debate, which is, as far as I am concerned, absolutely unacceptable. Is the police sufficiently equipped, I ask the Minister via the Speaker. We saw during the demonstrations that the police are currently using those carts to essentially remove people, but what if there are riots at night? We saw that in Amsterdam two weeks ago, but it also reminded me very strongly of New Year's Eve. Can the police adequately handle what they encounter, especially at night and also with those ever-changing—as I would like to call them—modi operandi of rioters? As far as my party group is concerned, the goal is crystal clear: we want a visible, approachable, and accessible police force. Our police force is a jack-of-all-trades. And that is actually just as well, because that service desk is not closing, and the desk is not closing for anyone either, so everyone can turn to the police at any time, and must be able to do so. But a jack-of-all-trades also means: continuing to mop while the tap is running. I feel responsible for repairing that tap on a few points. And in this regard, I am thinking of football; I have submitted a motion for that. I am thinking of demonstrations. We will be discussing that in the House. And I am thinking of persons with confused behaviour. We received a cabinet response this week regarding the committee's exploratory study, and I look forward to the debate on that. Chair. The police also have a task. The police budget has risen considerably over the past seven years, yet everyone still complains about a lack of police, and it is also difficult for some to have that police truly accessible or within reach. If you look at the findings of the Court of Audit this week, it mentions that 31% of the total budget goes to investigation. In the questions for the written round, we discuss an operational strength comprising eight types of work. If you calculate all that on a dreary afternoon, it turns out there are 20,000 operational personnel. I then wonder: are they all actually working shifts? So I am just saying that the budgets do not actually provide a crystal-clear overview of how many police officers are currently on the street, even for the purpose of conducting a rational discussion about it. Because, of course, there is always a shortage, but when is it actually as we agreed upon? I would very much like to have more insight into that. And also into the overhead percentages; because when you look at the figures now, that seems like a lot to me—a high percentage. Then back to those core tasks of the police. As far as I am concerned, that is simply working shifts, both in enforcement and in investigation.

Chair. I already said it during the committee debate: this cabinet is more likely to build a hundred castles in the air than a single nuclear power plant. But just imagine that they were to proceed with the construction of a single nuclear power plant after all, in Zeeland; then I have brought the following motion, which is not exactly concise. This is co-signed by colleagues Flach and Bontenbal of the Confessional bloc. The House, having heard the deliberations, considering that the Central Government designated a preferred location in the municipality of Borsele in December 2022 for the possible construction of two nuclear power plants and that the Central Government announced in February 2024 that it would adopt a provisional Central Government-Regional Package simultaneously with the provisional location decision; noting that the first advisory letter from the Central Government-Regional Package Zeeland Area Coordinator of December 2024 indicates that there are significant bottlenecks, such as tensions with other spatial initiatives, a lack of coherence and coordination between various projects, uncertainty regarding location and construction impact, and insufficient alignment with relevant stakeholders; whereas an integrated approach is essential to address the aforementioned bottlenecks and to realize a broad-supported National-Regional Package; requests the Government: to draw up a regional vision, in order to promote continued support for and confidence in new nuclear power plants in Zeeland, in which spatial initiatives, including the construction of nuclear power plants, are coordinated in an integrated manner; to establish a steering group with the relevant authorities to expeditiously draw up a broad-supported National-Regional Package together with the region and regional liaison Knops, so that this can be adopted simultaneously with the provisional location decision, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I believe I just did that as well. Certainly regarding that periodic evaluation of the minimum wage level, we have now discussed the reference values that I have enshrined in the draft ministerial regulation. However, you will also have to look at other aspects, such as competitive position and employer costs. That should also result in a comprehensive assessment of the minimum wage level. I think that brings me to the end of my first term.

If the motion remains as it stands, I hereby advise against it. The motion regarding document no. 2201, concerning "not cutting public services for investments in Defence": advised against. This is not in line with the framework agreements, particularly regarding no tax increases. This is to avoid preempting discussions we are still having regarding funding for Defence and Ukraine. The motion regarding document no. 2202 concerns a long-term financial strategy for defence expenditures. This relates to the agreement with the VVD motion. If I may interpret the motion as such, the answer is also the same. In the run-up to the NATO summit, we will first examine what else is needed for Defence. We will inform your House prior to the NATO summit. In that case, this motion receives the House's judgment.

Thank you, Chair. Thanks also to the State Secretary for the answers and to my colleagues for the debate. I believe that together we are taking steps in the right direction regarding a very important subject, namely work for everyone. However, the House of Representatives is not yet always equally accessible to everyone regarding inclusivity and accessibility in the workplace. For example, I cannot read out my motion myself due to my visual impairment. We have resolved this very pragmatically: I would like to submit a motion via the Chair.
