The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that employers encounter unnecessary regulations and bureaucracy when hiring people with a distance to the labour market; noting that work and/or transport provisions are only granted once an employment contract has been concluded; considering that a work provision can be of essential importance as an aid in the search for work; considering that for certain disabilities, a transport provision or mobility training is necessary to be able to apply for a job or start an internship; considering that people with a disability affecting their ability to work must be able to easily access the right aids, even without an employment contract; whereas the threshold for a company to hire someone with a distance to the labour market is lowered when a potential employee is already in possession of a (conditional) allocation of his or her necessary aids; requests the Government to review the allocation of work and/or transport facilities so that potential employees with a distance to the labour market can find a job sooner, and to inform the House about this in the third quarter of 2025, and proceeds to the order of the day.

You know, what I am really concerned about is that we are now trying to drive huge... not marker stakes here, because that fits in with the norming that the Volt party leader also often speaks about. I am concerned that we are pushing the debate in completely the wrong direction. If I say that it is important for institutions to treat each other respectfully, then I believe I am being clear. If I have said that I support the mayor, and have also spoken about institutions in doing so, I believe I am being clear as well.

This cabinet relies on the strength of diplomacy and knows it is supported in this by many, many countries that are continuously increasing joint pressure on Israel to ultimately reach a ceasefire, to ensure that humanitarian aid can take place, to ensure that we can once again embark on the path to a sustainable solution together regarding Gaza, and when it comes to strengthening Lebanon and dismantling Hezbollah.

Yes, finally. A committee debate on poverty and debt was scheduled. As far as I understand, that has been replaced because we are going to discuss the Participation Act in plenary session. In the meantime, a very worrying report has been released stating that financial concerns among young people—though this applies to broader target groups—are increasing. I consider it important, especially now, to hold a debate with the House regarding what financial concerns and debt entail. I am also requesting this because the committee debate we were supposed to hold together is no longer on the agenda.

Chair. There is undoubtedly a learning curve. New policy may also be introduced, so that makes things easier as well. The motion regarding document no. 999 concerns access to restorative justice provisions. In my view, restorative justice, including mediation and restorative mediation, is currently insufficiently utilized. I am working on various initiatives to improve this. The Minister of Justice is primarily responsible for prosecution policy. In that context, I will discuss this point with him. We will return to this in the restorative justice progress letter, which is expected to be sent in the first quarter of 2025. On that basis, I arrive at the assessment of "deferring".

One more remark, precisely in that light. I am pleased that the State Secretary is providing this addition, because our abortion legislation is a balance between, on the one hand, the freedom a woman is given and, on the other, unborn life, which cannot speak for itself. We all know that for everyone, regardless of their perspective, abortion is an incredibly profound decision and that one faces an enormous dilemma when one is unintentionally pregnant. That is precisely why I would like to ask the State Secretary not to think solely within the framework of the right to self-determination, but also from the perspective of: what does this era need, what do those young teenage mothers who suddenly discover they are pregnant need, and what do people who repeatedly experience this situation need? How can we ensure that we reduce these kinds of situations instead of immediately speaking in frames where one side is solely in favor of the right to self-determination and the other solely in favor of unborn life? The State Secretary speaks of the debate, but this is such a vulnerable, sensitive issue that I wish to engage in a dialogue as much as possible about how we can at least take small steps forward in this regard. I would like to encourage the State Secretary in this, also in his preparation for the debate on November 13. This is a theme I always speak about with hesitation, I notice. For I know that I am talking about the pain of others who cannot speak here. Therefore, I want to urgently request the State Secretary not to too easily disregard that objective of the previous cabinet, of which Prime Minister Schoof also said: precisely because we know the background to it, we will once again look at how it should be followed up. He did not make a firm commitment in this regard. I do not want to put words in the State Secretary's mouth. Sometimes I do that with a political maneuver on other fronts, but not here. On this point, I really want to ask him to handle this very carefully, because it is a very delicate issue.

It is always good to have certain knowledge and facts so that you can intervene as targeted as possible. If the NCTV states year after year that antisemitism exists in all layers of society but is more dominant among the far right, the far left, and radicalized Muslims in the Netherlands, then you have to do something with those facts. Then you have to consider how we can intervene in a targeted manner to combat antisemitism there as effectively as possible. I am not opposed to that at all. But what I do have a problem with is that over the past few days we have once again created an atmosphere as if there is one big integration problem in the Netherlands, because that is how hundreds of thousands of Dutch people perceive the tone and the statements they have heard from political leaders in recent days. If you are a third- or fourth-generation Dutch person, you don't want to be addressed about that anymore. Then you want there to be people here in The Hague who will stand by you as a Dutch person with a migrant background and say: I am not judging you based on, or accusing you of, the criminal behavior of the scum that wreaked havoc in Amsterdam. We, as the majority, therefore also have the responsibility to ensure that this large minority group of Dutch people with a migration background is included, so that they can live together with us. So yes, you can intervene in a targeted manner from time to time, but that requires very careful language and tone from the politicians here in The Hague.

Agreed. That is why I refer once again to our comprehensive package of reforms. If the Cabinet is of the opinion that simplification means leaving the child budget untouched or perhaps requires adjustment in light of the broader system change, then that conclusion must be reached. My motion concerns the fact that the answer contains at least the beginning of a direction, particularly regarding the healthcare allowance. I just indicated in my speech that I see a trend that I believe is highly undesirable, namely that the healthcare allowance is being made more important during a period when, in my view, we as the House are saying that it should be made less important. Therefore, I would like an answer from the Cabinet members regarding how they intend to do this in light of the overall system change. This does not have to be done all at once, but we as a party consider a long-term vision, a direction, and the third term to be very important.

I fear I am repeating myself, but as far as I am concerned, that is to truly underscore the clarity on this point every single time. Everyone in this cabinet is convinced that racism, discrimination, and replacement theories have no place whatsoever in this cabinet's policy. If people have said different things at other times, that is not up to me as Prime Minister, with a team that started yesterday. I consider it essential to ensure that we can be held accountable for cabinet policy, for what we do as a cabinet. Hold us accountable every day for what we do.

We have sufficient vision, and we are speaking with other European colleagues about how to deal with a Trump administration. I am really looking forward to engaging in megaphone diplomacy regarding the Trump administration here. I could go around saying "this or that is a disgrace" every day, but I focus primarily on what is effective. It is effective to be in dialogue with European partners now, as the Minister for Foreign Trade is currently doing in Warsaw, and to consider how to prepare further should trade measures be introduced. This applies to other policy areas as well. It is effective to coordinate within the EU and consider how to respond when the need arises in that regard, rather than immediately laying all cards on the table in public.

Mr. Chairman. In just a few weeks, this is already the third debate in which we discuss the Russian war of aggression in Ukraine, all the geopolitical developments influencing it, and the question of what the Dutch position and commitment should be. Once again, it is an important debate in view of the European Council later this week. It is entirely logical that not everyone in this House held a unanimous opinion regarding all the events of the past few weeks and the proposals currently on the table in Brussels. Today, too, we hear differences in tone and assessment. Today, too, the necessary critical questions have been asked and comments made, to which I will try my best to do justice in my first term. We will continue to have that conversation. And we must, for this is a time of major decisions, and these can only be discussed and ratified in one place: here, in the heart of our democratic system. Given the nature of the matter, the debate then very quickly turns to where opinions differ; there are no debates without discussion. Nevertheless, at the start of my first term, I would like to emphasize once again how important it is that we keep the bigger picture in mind, looking beyond differences of view. For as I hear it again today, the House and the Cabinet grosso modo remain in agreement on a few fundamental matters: that Ukraine deserves our continued support, that this is also in the interest of our own national security, and that Europe will have to do more in the military field to strengthen its own defense. For me and the Cabinet, these are the cornerstones of the field. Within that framework, I will once again articulate the Dutch position later this week in Brussels, including the legitimate financial concerns raised today and last week by a number of parliamentary groups. Mr. Speaker. My response is structured along the following lines: a block on Ukraine, a block on European defense, a block on the Middle East, and then a block on other important topics. It has just become known that President Trump and Putin spoke to each other by telephone. Given that two major players are speaking here, this is by definition an important event. It could be a first important step. The next step must be a complete ceasefire. And then on the way to the peace plan. It is also important to note that Ukraine and Europe were not included in this. That is a factual observation. Incidentally, I consider it important to first know exactly what has been proposed. However, that does not alter the fact that our support for Ukraine continues unabated. One of the questions asked, I believe by Ms. Yeşilgöz, was the following: The US spoke with Ukraine and Russia. When will Europe finally come to the table? For this Cabinet, it is essential that Ukraine, but also the European partners, sit at the table during the negotiations. After all, this is also about our security. We make this clear in all discussions with international partners. The Netherlands is at the table, digitally again last weekend with a number of important European countries, and incidentally again this week. The US Secretary of State, Rubio, also indicated this week that the EU must be involved in the peace talks. But raising this point is not enough. European allies, including the Netherlands, must also bring something to the table. That is precisely what we are doing, by participating in military planning and by continuing our support for Ukraine—morally, politically, financially, and militarily—unwavering. Mr. Van Baarle asked: can the Minister elaborate on the new commitments within the coalition of the willing? Can the Minister guarantee that the Netherlands will not preempt military participation in Ukraine? The outlines of a peace agreement remain largely unclear. We are working hard to fill in these parts of the picture as much as possible. In that context, it is important that the Netherlands sits at the table with planners within that so-called coalition of the willing to map out and develop options. It is beneficial for our information position and good to have a say in steering this process. This is consistent with our committed role. We are doing this out of the urgency to play a leading role from within Europe to ensure our security. Moreover, this is also the kind of responsibility that the United States expects of us. Planning does not automatically mean that a decision has been made regarding a contribution. We have a mandate to participate in discussions with the coalition of the willing regarding the outlines of the security guarantee. And yes, a number of countries, as Prime Minister Starmer said, have already indicated within the framework of the coalition of the willing that they will contribute militarily. However, a military contribution requires a political decision. Parliament will be fully involved in this. The Cabinet attaches importance to providing your House with careful information. Agreements have been made regarding this as well. This means that, with regard to the Netherlands, no concrete commitments were made last Saturday without your consent, and no concrete commitments will be made in the coming days either.

I was still working on the motion regarding document no. 662. I said that, with all due respect to Member Eerdmans, I found the motion somewhat vague. It refers to the transfer of direct investment power to the European Commission. This is not at all on the agenda. The powers are defined in the treaties. I have already indicated in the debate that there are no intentions to amend the treaties. I point out that we already have instruments such as the European Defence Fund and that the Cabinet is looking into whether money can be used more intelligently, for example through joint procurement of defence equipment. For this, I envision an intergovernmental route rather than a Community one via the Commission. One could say that the motion is superfluous. But when I look at the texts, I also find it somewhat suggestive. Therefore, I would advise against it. Again, it is not on the agenda, but based on what I see here, it is advised against with this explanation.

In my assessment, I try to do justice to all the remarks made here. But if I am asked to anticipate 'if-then' scenarios in the spring, I say that I am not going to do that. I have said that if we start drawing plans from this now, we will have a funding issue again. That broadens the problem. In the search for a solution, we are trying to reach a consensus here. I think these remarks exacerbate the problem once again. So let me take a moment to give a proper assessment of the motion. Then we can see if we can reach a mutual understanding. Just so you can make your agenda item, Chair. I think the outstanding questions can be dealt with fairly quickly. I still have a few VAT questions, but we have discussed most of them. I also have a small block of miscellaneous items. I think that can be done fairly quickly, also bearing in mind that we have enjoyed the main point of the meal.

Naturally, we are seeking support from a number of European countries regarding our migration policy. We are working in various ways, for example with Denmark and Italy, to see how we can take further steps in this regard and also change policy in Europe together. We will do the same with regard to the opt-out. But at the same time, I also say to Mr. Wilders—and he will undoubtedly agree with me—that the Cabinet's priority at this moment lies with the measures we must take today. We are seeking that balance together, also knowing, as Mr. Wilders said, that a treaty amendment is necessary and that this cannot be arranged in an afternoon.

Chair. Developments in Syria are escalating rapidly. This has consequences, partly due to the involvement of Turkey and Iran, and possibly Israel and Iraq. I believe this calls for a political debate, in part to determine our position as the House. Therefore, I would like to hold a plenary debate on this matter soon.
I would like to keep the option open for a two-minute debate prior to the NATO ministerial and OSCE meetings.

I share Mr. Wilders' condemnation of what happened in Amsterdam. There was indeed antisemitic violence in Amsterdam at night. But if we are serious about combating antisemitism broadly, then we cannot stop at condemning antisemitism that exists within Islamic circles. We must also look at antisemitism in radical left-wing circles, in radical right-wing circles, and in all sorts of other places where it occurs. If Mr. Wilders dismisses it so easily—that when he stands on a stage in Budapest with a Prime Minister of Hungary who uses antisemitism as a campaign theme in every election campaign, when he stands on a stage with a Mr. Kickl who spews antisemitic filth every time—then he must also take the responsibility to call those people out on it. Mr. Wilders, your credibility stands or falls with your willingness to denounce all forms of antisemitism and to work towards concrete solutions. I really want to ask you for a response to that.

Those were the questions. I will now move on to the motions. I will start with the motion on document no. 751 by Ms. Westerveld of GroenLinks-PvdA regarding a practical education check, if I may call it that. I strongly endorse your call to properly include practical education when developing new policy, just as applies to other types of schools. As far as I am concerned, we will do so, but I would like to approach it in a pragmatic way, without setting up all sorts of grandiose measures. The call is clear: try to take into account the impact of policy on practical education at the outset, so that you do not have to fix everything at the back end. If Ms. Westerveld gives me the leeway to approach this pragmatically, I will pass this motion to the House.

Thank you, Chair. Naturally, we wish everyone the opportunity to get and keep a fulfilling job, but for everyone, that is not a given. When it comes to people with a distance to the labor market — that is actually a misphrase, as the labor market is distanced from them — the Jobs Agreement has taken good steps to increase the chance of employment. The VVD can therefore support this simplification, but we do have a number of points of concern. That is why we have chosen to de-hammer this bill. Much is needed to truly achieve an inclusive labor market. I am looking in particular at employers, who should and could do much more, but also at the government, which must create better preconditions to make the step towards work easier, and at the target group itself, which leaves talents untapped, no matter how difficult it may be. I will start with employers. There is still too often a fear of the unknown. Especially with the current tightness in the labor market, with which we are painfully confronted daily, opportunities must be utilized much better. I therefore expect more from employers. I am pleased to see that, regarding the Jobs Agreement, it is precisely the business sector that is performing well—performing better than the government. This is explicitly one of the focus points we have within the VVD. We find it remarkable that the government is lagging behind. I have raised this point frequently in recent years. As far as I am concerned, you cannot impose a quota obligation on the business sector if the government itself is lagging behind. If the government does not set a good example itself, if it does not hire people with a distance to the labor market and give them the opportunities they deserve, how can it impose an obligation on the business sector? I am very curious about the State Secretary's vision on this. My colleague Paulusma from D66 and I have an amendment regarding this, which she will explain. The second focus point for us concerns work accommodations and also transport accommodations. If you have a specific disability, these accommodations can be important for being able to take the step towards employment at all. It still happens too often that a work accommodation is only granted once an employment contract has been concluded. However, the moment you are looking for a job, you need precisely that necessary tool in your search for work or to secure an internship. I think it is wise to critically examine how we handle the allocation of work accommodations and transport provisions. In certain situations, this could also include mobility training. It seems sensible to me not to wait for the employment contract, but to help the individual who has the ambition to seek a job or find an internship beforehand by granting these types of provisions in a timely manner. By doing so, we eliminate bureaucracy and obstacles for employers, who do not need to be involved in the application process. I am very curious to hear the State Secretary's views on this matter.

Mr. Flach is a very good speaker, so I sensed that point. One of the reasons in 2018, as I read in the documents, was the difference between the pedagogical requirements we impose on childcare centers and on childminders. At the time, there were questions in the House because it is strange that we require this on the one hand, but not on the other. I was curious how Mr. Flach views that point.

For the Netherlands, if I have listened correctly in London and at other meetings over the past few days, one thing is clear. One: in the short and medium term, it cannot be done without the Americans. Two: we want to do it with the Americans, because we simply need them desperately to ensure that they continue to guarantee the security of Europe and the Netherlands within the NATO framework. Yes, we see that the Americans are taking different positions and hold different views. We also see that they are very emphatically pushing Europe to do more itself. We believe that we must take up that challenge. But that is not aimed at doing it without the Americans. I think that this is not the moment to hold that discussion either. In that case, we would be having a very different debate. We must first ensure that we achieve peace in Ukraine and that, regarding the defense industry and everything related to defense in Europe, we can stand on our own two feet more. The transatlantic bond is important. If it is broken by America, then we may have to enter into a debate about how to deal with that. But at this moment, our position is simply: with America.

That 700 million was existing money. Or rather, existing Defense money. I must put that politely. That money is pinpointed to the drones, because there has also been a very explicit request from Ukraine to ensure that we spend our money on that. I was able to promise that to Mr. Zelensky today.

This would almost require a separate debate or a seminar at Clingendael, I say to Mr. Van der Burg. It is true, of course, that the geopolitical context seems to be gaining importance in the eyes of many. For years, the Palestinian cause was about the Palestinian cause. I believe Arafat prioritized that in 1968. Before that, the Arab countries concerned themselves with that issue. At that time, it was primarily the Palestinians, the Palestinian leaders, the PLO leadership, and the like, with all the pros and cons. At this moment, the geopolitical dimension has clearly increased. The geopolitical dimension is actually also underscored by the fact that the American president is currently visiting the Gulf States and is viewing the Israeli-Palestinian conflict partly through a geopolitical lens.

Regarding those eight countries. The Dublin Convention applies to EU countries, but it is true: before you eventually reach the Netherlands from Greece, you pass through quite a few EU countries. European law simply works in such a way that you must be registered in a country. Then the Dublin Convention applies. It is true: a number of those people — in fact, it concerns the majority of those people — are not registered anywhere. I think that was also one of the topics of discussion regarding the European Migration Pact. At the same time, I say that no pact would have been concluded if Greece, Italy, and Spain could not count on solidarity from the other European countries regarding the reception of asylum seekers.

I understand Mr. Ceder's temptation to link various matters together, but they genuinely have nothing to do with each other, so I am approaching them separately. I reiterate that I consider MDT a wonderful initiative. I have spoken with many participants. I also speak with our own civil servants who are committed to it with full passion and for whom the choices we are making now are also very painful. At the same time: if I am forced to make choices within tight budgetary constraints, I will make the choices as they currently stand in the budget. These relate to raising the standard of basic skills, tackling the teacher shortage and the shortage of school leaders, safeguarding social safety, and all such matters. At the same time, I have stated that I want to continue safeguarding the value of MDT that we have experienced. I am personally committed to this by sitting down with civil society organizations and the business community. In this way, we can keep the lessons we have learned, as well as the partnerships that have emerged, alive as much as possible.

Chair. I thank the members of your House for the exchange of views we were able to have on a sensitive and charged subject. As I said: if Jewish people are not safe in our society, then something is fundamentally wrong in our society. We all have a collective responsibility to keep all our fellow Dutch citizens safe. Particularly in higher education, it is important that we can speak with one another and sometimes even clash, but that we always do so in a respectful manner. Chair. We are working on a number of measures. More measures are forthcoming. The task force that is about to start will once again present new proposals for measures. In this way, we want to ensure more and more that Jewish and Israeli students and staff feel safe at the university. This means that I can essentially only give the House a judgment regarding motion No. 159 by Mr. Eerdmans, which is widely supported by others as well, and which requests me to remove all barriers so that Jewish and Israeli students and staff can attend their university without fear. I cannot interpret it any other way. Removing all barriers is an enormous ambition, but I have no choice but to submit this motion to the House's judgment.

To begin the elaboration of the motion, the Cabinet believes we need to know the direction to take, more specifically than simply stating that no tax is being paid. This requires clarity regarding what is actually intended and whether or not grossing up is taking place. This is a truly important question to ask before initiating the entire process, also due to all the legislative and other implications. The Cabinet’s assessment is that if we wish to navigate the path of amending the tax exemption and everything associated with it, including the two-thirds majorities required for this, the House must express its opinion on the matter, as this helps in developing the proposal.

The capacity for reflection is a wonderful quality that I wish for many of us. We in the Cabinet will absolutely do so ourselves in the coming period, including all suggestions that have been made here in the House and will undoubtedly be made in the future. Chair. I will proceed to the next "block"; that is what I believe it is called. This concerns the physical living environment. With that, I have concluded the economics block. I begin with Mr. Timmermans, who asked whether we cannot accelerate the energy transition and whether subsidies are not going to the wrong parties. He asked why the Cabinet is not making neighborhoods with many drafty houses more sustainable. I do not think the funds from the Climate Fund are going to the wrong parties. They are actually being deployed to make homes and business premises more sustainable, to help people and small business owners, and to ensure lower energy bills, with particular attention to low and middle incomes. This is addressed in more detail in the coalition programme. Mr. Eerdmans asked me about the status of the natural gas-free plans. Is the Cabinet going to proceed with those unfeasible plans, which cost 300 billion? Let me begin by saying that those figures do not seem familiar to me, at least. Making homes natural gas-free and sustainable is part of the energy transition. The Cabinet is proceeding with this in a sensible and feasible manner. Mr. Omtzigt asked me how the Cabinet intends to implement the energy transition. That is a very broad question. The Cabinet will proceed expeditiously. In doing so, it is also important that the policy is sustainable, feasible, and implementable. It will not surprise you that the Cabinet will elaborate further on the implementation of the agreements from the Framework Agreement in the coalition programme. Mr. Omtzigt also asked me when the Housing Summit will take place, where pension funds, municipalities, provinces, and housing corporations make agreements for 100,000 homes per year, including 30,000 new social rental homes. The aim of the Framework Agreement is to organize the Housing Summit with the parties necessary to realize that housing construction target. Agreements are thereby made to structurally increase housing construction. The current expectation is that this will take place between autumn and Christmas, as preparing for such a summit also takes some time. Further clarification on this will be provided in the coalition programme. Mr. Omtzigt also asked me whether I will ensure that Brussels allows for an expansion of the possibilities for financial support for the construction of mid-market rental housing. In accordance with the Framework Agreement, this Cabinet will also commit to creating more scope within European state aid rules for financial support for the construction of mid-market rental housing. Ultimately, it is up to the European Commission to make a decision on this. I cannot therefore simply promise that we will arrange this, but I can promise that we will make every effort to achieve it. Mr. Stoffer asked me what the Cabinet will do concretely to ensure that young people, in particular, can find an affordable home again. According to the agreement, and therefore also for us as a Cabinet, reducing the severe housing shortage is a top priority. That is what the Minister of Housing, Spatial Planning and the Environment stands for, and consequently what the entire Cabinet stands for. The shortage is a hindrance to the livelihood security of young people in particular. Therefore, the Cabinet is strengthening its commitment to housing construction with a range of measures. Target groups, such as young people, are also taken into account. To ensure affordability, at least 30% of new construction must be social housing on average, and two-thirds of new construction must be affordable for low- and middle-income earners, through purchase or rental. The Cabinet is stimulating housing construction over the next five years with an annual boost of 1 billion euros, including for the construction of private rental homes.

This Prime Minister knows better than anyone that the decision-making must still take place in the Council of Ministers. There, all aspects are weighed. It will not be a frivolous decision by the Cabinet. All discussions, including tonight's, will play a role in this. But please first give the Cabinet the space to finalize the decision-making on this point. After that, you can enter into a discussion with the Cabinet. In my opinion, this does full justice to how democracy ought to function. During today's debate, I have tried in every way to ensure that your requests in the House are honored, so that the debate can take place in full openness.

With all due respect to Mr. Timmermans: we genuinely disagree on this. The unity of cabinet policy and the system make it possible for the Minister of the Interior and I to sign. In doing so, we make a broader assessment. Ms. Faber has endorsed this. Therefore, Ms. Faber does not need to sign to prove that there is unity of cabinet policy. That unity exists and it stands. Consequently, not a single volunteer need have felt, so to speak, shortchanged. That is probably not entirely proper Dutch anymore, I believe. But it is important that by doing so, we can do justice to the system and those volunteers in every way, and that we can also ensure that those volunteers feel fully supported in our society.

I summarize it in my own words as follows: the role of the examining magistrate is primarily one that encourages reflection on the investigation and its progress, but that the power to impose sanctions does not fit well with this. I say this partly in view of the inevitable possibility of submitting such a request at a later stage, certainly within the statutory time limit following the initiation of proceedings. Therefore, on this basis, I advise against this amendment.

Yes. I feel it is important to respond to this, because there are two errors in Ms. Teunissen's argument, and this is a delicate matter. After all, it is not up to the 'Triangle' (referring to the three-party consultation body) to determine where demonstrations are permitted. You are always allowed to demonstrate. We have frameworks in the Public Manifestations Act. One of them is traffic. In any case, regarding all the demonstrations that have taken place here on the highway, there was absolutely no prior consultation with the 'Triangle'. Therefore, they are neither prohibited nor permitted. I feel it is important to ask Ms. Teunissen the following one more time. I would like to know from her whether, in her view, the current legal framework for demonstrations is sufficient to safeguard, protect, monitor, or whatever word you wish to attach to the right to demonstrate.

Chair, thank you very much. I count three motions, which I appreciate as follows. The motion by Ms. Piri regarding document no. 2987 concerns not opening Cluster 3 for EU accession negotiations with Serbia. I, too, consider the element of the rule of law to be very important in policy. I refer to the Copenhagen Criteria regarding the rule of law. There is something to be said about this in Serbia. Furthermore, I consider it important that the dialogue between Belgrade and Pristina is progressing, also on Belgrade's part. I also consider the Common Foreign and Security Policy of the European Union to be important. Serbia is currently operating in line with this to 51%, whereas for Albania and North Macedonia, for example, this is 100%. This brings me to my appreciation of the House's judgment. I do not believe that at this moment, the time is right for the EU to open Cluster 3 with Serbia. It will not be easy to achieve this in the European Union in this manner, I add, but I am very committed to it. The second motion, regarding document no. 2988 by Mr. Paternotte, states that espionage is unacceptable and requests the government to commit to an investigation into it and to explicitly take the security situation into account. The latter is always done, incidentally, but it is regrettable that this apparently also has to happen with EU member states. With this, I arrive at the House’s judgment on this motion. Then the third motion. I have just been handed it, but of course I listened carefully when it was read out. That is the motion regarding document no. 2989 concerning Georgia. We are fully committed to pursuing all leads to ensure that the administration does not get away with its current conduct. Here too, if I read it correctly, I arrive at the House’s judgment of appreciation. With that, I have assessed the three motions. "Provide an appreciation," I should say.

I am happy to agree to that. That seems like a good suggestion to me. Then there is the motion regarding document no. 119. That concerns the Tromsø Convention. I would like to suggest to Member Flach that he postpone it, as the motion is currently premature. I will include the position on the Tromsø Convention in the legislative evaluation, which we will start at the end of this year, so that it can be weighed in the broader context of bottlenecks in the implementation of the Woo.

I think that is roughly stated in my job description. There are indeed no taboos. That is a line we have conveyed specifically to the civil servants for the benefit of the ministerial committee. At the same time, you have to make a political choice together. We have also made those political choices in the Framework Agreement and the Government Programme. These are important for this cabinet as well. They therefore indicate fairly clear boundaries within which we ultimately look at what we can do. Everyone can have their own opinion about that, but that is the political reality in which we operate. I think it is possible to really make progress within that political reality. I think that with the start letter we have now sent, we are providing an important impetus for this. We will continue working hard on this in the coming period. Even then, we will seek support every time. We do not do this only within the cabinet, and the most committed ministers do not do this only in the House, but we also do it with all parties in the field, however difficult that may be each time.

I will continue then, Chair. I was referring to a number of questions that I posed more or less rhetorically. But a concrete question for the Minister is whether, in relation to the last point—which concerned the indexation of the travel allowance for employed persons—he is willing to investigate whether there are other schemes for employed persons that are not indexed annually. I think it would be good to have insight into this; I have also seen a great deal in the media regarding people's travel expenses. Can the Minister indicate how and when agreements will be reached with the municipalities regarding the increase in municipal housing costs, as included in the framework agreement? Adjustments to the tax and benefits system will also be necessary—as I just mentioned—to ensure that working pays off and that working more pays off more. PwC's proposal to work with a progressive rate based on hourly wages and a regressive rate based on the number of working hours per week could be of interest in this regard. The State Secretary has already promised to take that into account, or weigh it, in his elaboration, but there may well be other very different ideas that achieve the same effect. I believe Mr. Vijlbrief is also open to seeking a solution to that problem within the new system. And can the Minister also indicate what we can already learn from the pilots in healthcare and education to make working more hours more attractive? Can the Cabinet look at what can be done with employers regarding more flexible scheduling and working hours, and also to provide more insight into what working more hours yields for someone, net, in terms of wages and pension—for example, on the payslip—so that people can make a better choice? Mr. Chairman. Cruyff said: you only see it when you understand it. That is why it is necessary to gain more insight into the financial situation of workers in our country. Poverty figures are now being reported on structurally. The VVD wants to challenge the Cabinet to also find a way to structurally formulate targets for full-time workers, the self-employed, and SMEs in a similar manner, and to include these—or better include them—in the purchasing power projections, so that this becomes a more prominent part of Cabinet decision-making.

Mr. Chairman. With a war on the European continent, with Putin doing whatever it takes to sabotage our stability within Europe as well, and with an American president pointing out our own responsibility, it is more important than ever that the European Union actually takes that responsibility. The self-evident nature of the American security umbrella is over. Although that may feel uncomfortable, the VVD simultaneously views this strategically as an opportunity. Too often, we have allowed ourselves to be lulled to sleep economically, financially, and in terms of NATO percentages. It is high time that the EU, as one of the three largest economic powers in the world, becomes a lion with teeth in the field of security as well. While we know that the NATO standard will eventually rise, all NATO allies must rapidly start meeting the current standard of 2%. For the VVD, it is entirely logical that the President of America holds all allies to that agreement. We believe that this discussion should also be conducted sharply within the Union, because it is inexplicable that large countries like Spain (1.28%) and Italy (1.49%) do not even exceed the 1.5% target. It is commendable that the Baltic states spend 3% of their gross domestic product on defense, but we also know that Spain and Italy would only really make a difference once they reach 2%. Geopolitical uncertainty simply does not allow for the negligence we are currently witnessing. Mr. Chairman, we must also consider how to proceed differently. A number of colleagues have already put forward proposals regarding this that we are less enthusiastic about. We are currently thinking primarily of a European standard, to support the existing NATO standard for defense spending per member state. We could make access to European funds conditional on this during the negotiations on the Multiannual Financial Framework. For what good are cohesion money and agricultural subsidies if Putin has his foot in the door? Is the Minister prepared to develop this idea? Mr. Chairman, we would certainly like to see the Prime Minister advocate during the Council for a proper division of tasks between the security responsibilities of NATO and those of the European Union. It is good that the European Commission has appointed a new Commissioner for Defence and that the European Parliament also has its own defence committee, but we really must prevent duplication of work. NATO has structures to establish military standards, determine capability targets, and jointly procure equipment. The EU, in turn, has the advantage of the internal market and the money. This can help to ramp up defence production within Europe at a much faster pace and to impose sanctions if external factors put our security under pressure. Does the Cabinet, together with the VVD, see the risks of overlapping roles and tasks? How are we going to prevent this? Not only must defence spending per Member State increase, but defence spending within the European Union must also increase. The Cabinet is rightly ambitious regarding the establishment of a robust defence industry policy, but what do joint procurement and better information exchange mean in practice? This has been debated frequently, but what steps will the Netherlands itself take? What obstacles exist? I would appreciate a response. Mr. Chairman. Just as NATO applies its military capabilities to safeguard our security, the EU can and must do the same in the area of the internal market. It is good that the Netherlands is pushing for a new sanctions package against Russia. The Minister rightly states that the reprehensible Russian shadow fleet constitutes their main source of income. What are we going to do about that? What actions do we foresee? This also applies to the circumvention of sanctions. Finally, Mr. Chairman. If we really want to put a stop to Putin, the consequences of his aggression must also be felt in Russia itself. Is the time perhaps ripe to proceed with the introduction of travel and visa restrictions against Russian tourists within the Schengen area? Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

It would be best if I answered now that I do not answer hypothetical questions, and we then proceed to the next interruption. I know that Mr. Boswijk is asking this because he is concerned about where things are heading. By "thing," I mean the NATO summit and our position within it. I have heard Mr. Vermeer, and actually Mr. Pool as well, say that they have questions regarding the substantiation. In all honesty: out of interest, I do have quite a few questions regarding the substantiation of the capacity targets, but that is different from wondering whether that substantiation exists. I am also submitting this motion together with my colleague Olger van Dijk, precisely to show you, Mr. Boswijk, and Mr. Paternotte that we are serious about this; otherwise, we would not be submitting this motion now. Our position is clear. Let's just see how things go in the coming weeks. But I think this motion clearly demonstrates what the stakes should be as far as we are concerned. There is no ambiguity involved.

I suspect that Mr. Van Baarle wishes to ask a question regarding the previous topic, so I would like to briefly finish the answer here. We are making it financially feasible to take on informal care by merging short-term and long-term care leave into informal care leave. We are raising awareness of the right to flexible working, increasing the child budget, and continuing, for example, the funding of free school meals. For most working parents, we are making childcare cheaper, with reimbursement no longer going through the parents. We are strengthening the social base and neighborhood networks to stimulate children's development and support parents in raising their children. As a result, families will need to rely less on youth care services.

I believe we are in complete agreement on that. Precisely clarity in this regard can ensure that we know where we are headed. Looking at the past, I see that such clarity has been present on several occasions. We have also requested a number of things from the House. Nevertheless, we always ask ourselves what is the best course of action in this regard. There are also many questions regarding the document, which I have asked in order to really bring a number of things to the surface. We can then proceed based on that. If you were to ask me what is important regarding periodic checks, I would say that I prefer them to be for four years. The moment it is stated that it will become five years because they cannot handle it otherwise, I am willing to move towards those five years, and possibly to five and a half or six. It is important to me that the Inspectorate returns to the classroom and that they can once again assess the quality of education in the classroom.

I had to laugh at first, because I thought it was about interrupting my own motion; I did not intend to interrupt myself there at all. I am getting to the point, for Mr. Van Nispen is asking an extraordinarily serious question. Today we are discussing this matter exclusively. If it has been included in the Criminal Code, under heavy pressure from this House of Representatives, that a ban on community service applies in specifically listed cases, then it is only logical that the relationship of that provision, which only applies to convictions, is also qualified when discussing summary penal orders. That is the direct background for raising only this one point right now, in the full certainty that we are going to have a substantive discussion about what else is and is not possible with summary penal orders. That is the reason why I am raising this here and now: because I believe it belongs here and now.

No, and this lady is certainly convinced of that. I belong to the "no words, but deeds" club, so those are also the proposals I make in that regard. That is the only part where I indicate that I really want to wait for it, because the VVD is also in favor of safety, which is also very important for children and for which the VVD has allocated funds and wants to do something. So I am not going to respond to that any other way than with this. Yes, it is quite a story.

This brings me to a few words of clarification regarding the character of the government team presenting itself to you and to the Netherlands today. For what is an extra-parliamentary cabinet? During the formation process and in the run-up to today, we have seen many different reflections on this. I deduce from this that it is difficult to provide a clear, hard definition. For the cabinet, the core of the concept of "extra-parliamentary" is that we will operate more independently from the parliamentary groups than is customary in our country, at a somewhat greater distance. Conversely, this means that the House can perform its oversight role better: without a coalition agreement negotiated down to the last detail, and consequently with less coalition pressure and more room for shifting majorities. In this way, there is more breathing room in the relationship between the House and the cabinet. If you ask me now exactly what that will look like in the coming period, I say, using a phrase from Ruud Lubbers: we will have to discover and shape that together "as we go along." As established in the final report by informateur Putters, an extra-parliamentary cabinet also requires a different composition, comprising the political leaders of the parliamentary groups that support this coalition in the House. It also requires cabinet members who have been selected primarily for their knowledge and experience, rather than solely for their pronounced party-political profile. I am personally an example of the latter, but not the only one. Surrounding me in Section K is a broadly composed team with extensive expertise, considerable societal experience, and—without exception—a deep commitment to the Netherlands; thus, not a black-and-white picture of political and apolitical ministers, but a balanced team. Mr. Chairman, I agree with everyone who has concluded over the past few months that the election result must, above all, be viewed as a call from the voter to the political establishment to do a number of things differently. The framework agreement *Hope, Guts and Pride* is the outcome of this, and at the same time, the framework within which the cabinet will operate. It is an agreement that very clearly shifts the course in a number of areas. I have already mentioned migration policy, agricultural and nature policy, and the proposals for better governance and a stronger democracy. It is also an agreement that rests on a foundation of sound public finances and financial stability. It has not escaped anyone's notice that the framework agreement already outlines quite detailed proposals on these subjects. That says something about the importance that the four parties that concluded the agreement attach to these topics. And I will simply state it very clearly: the Cabinet considers itself bound by this. These are important priorities for us as well. For many other subjects, the framework agreement leaves room for sensible proposals from both the House and the Cabinet. In most cases, those subjects are certainly no less urgent. The fact that fewer words are devoted to them in the framework agreement says nothing about the importance the Cabinet attaches to them. I would like to illustrate this using the frequently heard concept of "subsistence security," a concept that starts with income, poverty reduction, and purchasing power—also in the framework agreement. I mention the principle that work must pay off more, one of our most important priorities for the coming years. But subsistence security encompasses so much more. It is unthinkable without security, a basic need of every human being and every country, and an absolute core task of the government. It will not surprise you that I feel this way. But security of existence is also accessible and good healthcare, and education for everyone. It is a decent and affordable roof over your head, because housing is a basic need. For too many people, certainly for too many young people, owning a rental or purchased home is becoming an unattainable goal. We must turn the tide. Security of existence is a labor market that works for people, with sufficient security for employees and room for those seeking independence. It is also, for example, policy that ensures citizens and businesses can truly experience the energy transition, starting financially. In short, a range of important and diverse themes to work together on in the coming years, as Parliament and Cabinet, in connection with society. I hope that it will go this way, and frankly, I am counting on it a little, now that so many parties from left to right have made security of existence a central theme in the election campaign. Moreover, we can learn from history that this cabinet, too, will most likely face unexpected events and developments that no one can foresee at this moment. Even then, we will have to seek broad-based solutions together through consultation. I make this appeal to you today, with the promise on my part that the cabinet will reach out to all parliamentary groups in this House. Chair. There are three clear and important preconditions for the elaboration of the framework agreement: international, financial, and rule of law. Concerns were raised regarding the latter point. I want to be clear on this: this cabinet stands for the rule of law. You know that I have personally dedicated a large part of my working life to the protection of our rule of law. As a cabinet, we endorse the agreements that the four parties forming the government established on this matter at an early stage. This means that the rule of law is a given for us, with the most important anchors being the democratic freedoms and fundamental rights enshrined in our Constitution. We stand for this; we guarantee it. Anything more I might say about this now would detract from the absolute clarity I wish to offer everyone today. Today, I simply ask you and all the inhabitants of our country: judge us by what we are going to do; judge us by our actions. Internationally, the Netherlands is and remains a reliable partner within the EU and NATO, with the deep conviction that these two organizations are the cornerstone of our security and our prosperity. For me personally, it is perfectly clear that the protection of what we hold dear, of our freedoms, and of our entire way of life must be an absolute top priority in international security policy. And yes, defense is a crucial element in this. But a resilient society also requires strategic autonomy in other areas, such as energy, food security, and raw materials. We must not be naive about this. A horrific war is raging just a few hours' flight from here, in which a human life counts for nothing to the Russians. Our country, too, is dealing with foreign hacks and other attempts at espionage, infiltration, and foreign influence. Russia, China, the Middle East—instability lurks at Europe from all sides. The reality is that we must prepare ourselves for scenarios that were unthinkable for a long time. For that reason alone, a strong and prosperous Netherlands simply cannot operate in isolation. Nor will we do so. Ukraine can continue to count on Dutch support—financial, military, and political. The NATO defense quota of 2% will be met and legally enshrined. Furthermore, the Dutch government will abide by all international treaties bearing our signature, both within and outside the EU. Just as it should be. What we will no longer do is apply national weight to international agreements, for example in nature and climate policy—meaning no stricter interpretation of European rules than in other countries. We do not always have to be the best in class. We cannot afford that either, with so many people, so much economic activity, and such a high-quality agricultural and fisheries sector in such a relatively small country. What we *will* do is engage in dialogue within Europe wherever international agreements disproportionately affect our country or stand in the way of a reasonable solution. The Framework Agreement specifically mentions this option in the chapters on migration and agriculture. I know that many here, as well as many outside this room, categorize that as "impossible, cannot be done." I have been around long enough to realize that simply calling international agreements into question and discussing exceptional positions does not automatically lead to success. At the same time, we must clearly recognize that EU member states do indeed have the scope to determine for themselves how they wish to achieve certain goals. Therefore, a discussion on that is certainly possible. Moreover, I grew up in a family where hard work and perseverance were the norm. "Can't be done" is in the graveyard, and "won't" is next to it, I heard at home. So, are you receiving a 100% guarantee from the Cabinet today on this point that we will secure everything in Brussels? No, not that. But you do get a 100% promise that we will fight for Dutch interests and that we will find and utilize the available room. Regarding the final precondition, finances, I would like to say the following. The most important thing is that, in the best Dutch tradition, we opt for financial soundness. In other words: the national budget deficit remains within the agreed limit of 3%. Should the deficit appear to exceed that limit during the term, we will act accordingly. Not because Europe requires it, but to protect our children and grandchildren from excessive debt. We are not taking out a mortgage on the future. Unfortunately, it is a fact that this cabinet must keep a tighter grip on the purse strings than its predecessors. There are serious concerns regarding the national budget for both the short and long term. Trees no longer grow to the sky. And yet, we want to ensure that people have a little more money in their wallets and that the burden on businesses decreases. This inevitably means making choices, because in the national budget, surpluses are simply always offset by deficits. I refer to the VAT increases, the cuts to development aid, the abolition of national service, and the substantial interventions in the central government's civil service. There is a financial reality, and we must face it. The Cabinet cannot and will not shy away from that responsibility. And therein — finally, Chair — perhaps lies the answer to the question I have been asked most often in recent weeks. Why did you say "yes" to the request from the four parties to become Prime Minister? It is a position I never aspired to, a curious fate that unexpectedly crossed my path. And in all honesty: of course, it is also difficult to say "no" to such an honorable request. Nothing human is alien to me. But at my core, what motivates me is the public interest, for which I have dedicated myself heart and soul for 40 years. To be allowed to bear responsibility for that is truly the most beautiful thing there is. I could never have imagined that it would one day be in this place. But now that I stand here, no more important work is conceivable for me. In all the conversations I have had so far with the people around me in section K, I sense that same drive to bear that responsibility, to achieve something good for the Netherlands, given the election results of November 22, given the framework agreement, and given the scope available to translate good and sensible ideas into policy in close consultation with members of the House of Representatives and the Senate, with civil society organizations, and in connection with society. (Drums on the benches)

It is actually quite simple. Of course, you have to look further back at how much emissions have already been reduced. Even if you look at recent history, you see that from 2010 to 2018, emissions in those other sectors were reduced more than in agriculture. If you go back even further, you see that there have also been very large emission reductions in agriculture since 1990. The point is that the nitrogen challenge, alongside a number of fundamental adjustments to legislation, regulations, and permit granting—in which I generally support the Cabinet—will also be accompanied by emission reductions by all major economic players in our country. I think it is very important that this is done fairly, justly, and explainably, so that everyone contributes proportionally. I do not understand why, after Minister Van der Wal from the previous cabinet essentially started haggling over the percentages in my view by placing little on mobility, more on industry, and slightly more on agriculture... The motion by my colleague Boswijk and myself was crystal clear: everyone contributes equally, proportionally, so as a percentage. That was also Remkes' advice. He said: just make it 50%. We must restore that.

Fine. Then I will move on to the motions. The motion regarding document no. 2191 concerning not responding unfavorably to the outlines of the plan for the rearmament of Europe: judgment by the House. The motion regarding document no. 2195 concerning defense expenditure has many similarities with the motion regarding document no. 2202 from the ChristenUnie. I will return to that in a moment. As far as I am concerned, judgment by the House, if I may interpret the motion to mean that we first map out what else is needed for Defense in the run-up to the NATO summit and that we inform your House in good time before the NATO summit.

Ms. Bikker asks what pace the House can expect regarding the adjustment of the benefits system. This large-scale reform will take time; we must be honest about that. At the same time, I agree with Ms. Bikker that people cannot really wait for the reforms, which will take several cabinet terms to complete. That is why the Cabinet is making proposals for the long, medium, and short term. We will undoubtedly discuss the exact timeline in more detail soon, regarding when we will implement which reform. Mr. Bontenbal asks where the countercyclical …

I hope that the Dutch people judge us primarily on the results we achieve, and not—at least certainly not at this moment—on our view of humanity. I hope that they truly judge us on our results. In response to an interruption, I just mentioned that we operate strongly based on the trust people must have in one another. We in this cabinet must also be able to have just as much trust in one another. I also hope that the House and the Cabinet can conduct the debate with each other in such trust.

In my opinion, I must appreciate the motion. It seems to me that this should be discussed at the moment the House discusses the proposals. If I cannot judge the motion to be untimely, I will therefore advise against it. Then, the motion on document no. 2241 by Mr. Omtzigt regarding taking action so that people linked to ISIS are prosecuted for their crimes. The motion contains too many elements which we cannot at all judge or foresee at this moment whether we can actually follow up on. That raises too many questions. Although I naturally cannot disagree with the aim of the motion, I must really advise against it at this moment. The motion on document no. 2242 concerns the snapback sanctions regarding Syria. I leave that to the House's judgment. The possibility of reinstating sanctions is always present, should there be developments that warrant it. Mr. Chairman, that concludes my discussion.

Chair. After one month of Trump II, the world stands at a tipping point. Putin and Trump want to decide the future of Ukraine and Europe without Europe and Ukraine. It is bizarre, and it completely turns the law-based world order upside down. At the same time, we face a very clear choice: do we stand by and watch, or does Europe take matters into its own hands for the safety and freedom of our free society? Two weeks ago, I was in Ukraine with my colleague Paternotte and the Protect Ukraine foundation. It was a trip that made a deep impression on me. It is a war that inflicts very deep wounds on society, where people check the air raid siren with a special app as if it were a weather radar. I am impressed by Ukrainians who are constantly looking for ways to survive and contribute something to the fight against the Russians. One of the encounters that made the biggest impression on me was a conversation with 19-year-old Victoria. Her father, Volodymyr, was an electrician before the war, but after the Russian invasion, he voluntarily joined the army to fight on the front lines for his family and his country. He was killed on that front. That naturally leads to sadness, but Victoria told me: I am also proud; my father is a hero because he wanted to keep us safe. She told me this at the Heroes' Cemetery in Lviv, where fallen soldiers are buried every day while excavators are digging new graves nearby, because that war continues to this day, despite fine words from Trump and Putin and talks in Saudi Arabia. It is the story of millions of Ukrainians who have lost family members, have relatives living in occupied territories, and are now wondering if the world is going to abandon them while they make the greatest sacrifices for their own freedom and the freedom of Europe. For that is what we must unequivocally choose. This is not just a Ukrainian conflict; it is a European conflict. We must not abandon Ukrainians. The Americans and the Russians are already at the table in Riyadh. Those talks directly affect our own security. We must therefore take action. D66 calls on the Cabinet: immediately provide an additional package of emergency aid for Ukraine. We are therefore not calling for the continuation of that support, but for its intensification. Let Putin pay for it himself, with his own frozen assets. After all, every square kilometer that Ukraine can reclaim now makes it stronger at the negotiating table. Two. Increase defense investments, to at least 3% as far as D66 is concerned. Trump is right on one point: it is absurd that we as Europeans cannot protect our own continent. That is an insult to the taxpayer. Three. Opt for a European army, because if the Americans withdraw, this is the moment to be capable, as a serious global power, of keeping our own continent safe. Finally, I have a number of questions for the Prime Minister. What does Ukraine urgently need now, and what additional steps can the Netherlands take? What is the Cabinet's position regarding having the EU itself sit at the table during the peace negotiations? Does the Cabinet support the call for Ukraine's accelerated accession to the European Union? Does the Cabinet still fully support Ukrainian NATO membership? Is the Cabinet now also opting for moving towards at least 3% of defense spending and building a European army? Finally, Mr. Chairman, is the Cabinet prepared to constructively consider participation in a potential peacekeeping force if there is broad support for it in this House of Representatives, even if the PVV does not dare to choose that path?

It is a macabre coincidence that we are launching our action plan on antisemitism just as antisemitic violence flared up in Amsterdam last Thursday night. However, this did lead to the talks with Jewish civil society organizations and the discussion in the Council of Ministers yesterday resulting in a tightening that is very welcome to the Minister of Justice and Security, allowing us to incorporate current events with greater urgency into what we intend to do with the action plan in the coming period. I assume that you will also see these aspects reflected in the action plan. The Minister of Justice and Security will also say a number of things about this action plan shortly.

Mr. Flach is asking too much of me regarding this specific amendment, but I will give him that second chance for the second term, so I promise. My remark about Code Red referred to a broad socio-economic analysis. I have tried to substantiate this with electoral examples. Throughout Western society, you see the combination—I have called it a "toxic cocktail"—of rising inflation, low labor productivity, lower growth, and, in particular, marginal tax pressure. Together, this creates an enormous amount of uncertainty. People used to feel: "I just work, so maybe I can go on vacation once a year. I am getting ahead. My children are doing better. I can take steps forward in life." But now, even very normal people who simply have a normal job say that they are only concerned with keeping their heads above water. I also just outlined examples of people asking themselves: "Why am I still working? What should I be working for? It yields nothing." I have tried to capture that sentiment in "Code Red". For me, that is indeed a motivation to shout bold words about "work must pay off," but that is also my opinion. We truly believe that we need to pursue that full steam ahead. As I just said, as far as we are concerned, this budget is a great first step in that direction.

No, there isn't really a question for me in this interruption by Ms. Van der Plas either. I am willing to allow myself one factual correction, however. She only volunteered for this debate when Prime Minister Schoof returned from Apeldoorn. Otherwise, her colleague Vermeer would have led this debate. What she said outside the House were big words. She initially did not intend to discuss this here in the House as well. I am glad she is here now. Then we can see what consequences she attaches to the words she spoke outside the House. We will hear that when she gets to her contribution.

I would really like to tell Ms. Bikker that she, and indeed the entire House, can hold me accountable for this: that it is not just about the implementation of an agreement, but that there are other aspects involved as well. This cabinet truly stands for everyone and also stands for the fact that racism, discrimination, and exclusion can in no way be part of this cabinet policy.

In my view, the core of what I am arguing here is that we made the basic agreement with sensitivity to those implementation practices, an agreement which I believe was also supported by the House of Representatives. After all, we have to move past the point where we are going to enforce the law again. For the law is clear in itself. However, we want to do this with sensitivity. We do this by thinking along with the sectors, by providing insight into the problems, by clarifying dilemmas, and by initiating enforcement leniently. In my opinion, that is the line we also gathered from the House of Representatives.

Much has already been said, Chair. What I say, I may do partly on behalf of the Party for the Animals. It is always wonderful to see private member's bills being discussed, which is ultimately the highest and by far the most labor-intensive piece of parliamentary craftsmanship. It concerns a subject that has been in the political spotlight for years and that is close to the hearts of many people; that is also evident from the number of people still sitting here. Victims share their often very moving stories about what happened to them, or rather, what was done to them. At D66, the freedom to be visibly yourself is held in high regard. As a social liberal, I myself draw much inspiration from John Stuart Mill's adage, from *On Liberty*, which reads: the only liberty worthy of the name is the liberty to strive to increase our own well-being, as long as we do not attempt to rob others of theirs, or hinder their attempts to obtain it. In English, it is even more beautiful. Conversion therapies convey something that stands diametrically opposed to that. They are essentially saying: if you are like that, you are not allowed to be yourself, and we will do everything we can to ensure you toe the line. This is not about a superficial trait; it is about who you are and who you love. D66 stands firmly for the freedom to determine that for yourself. Anneke Goudsmit, from the very first D66 parliamentary group, was at the forefront of the abolition of Article 248bis of the Criminal Code back then. Boris Dittrich did the same regarding the opening up of marriage. Following preparatory work by Boris van der Ham, Vera Bergkamp added "sexual orientation" to Article 1 of the Constitution. And always, as is the case here, in cooperation with other parties. A broad front to anchor individual rights and freedoms. I also have great respect for the others, but in doing so, Ms. Paulusma stands in a fine tradition that I am proud of. Chair. I also wanted to talk about trustworthy politics, but I think we have covered that sufficiently. As spokesperson for Justice, I always follow discussions regarding the addition of offences to the Criminal Code with particular interest. Inevitably, the same questions recur: is there a problem that justifies government intervention; what options already exist; and how does the nature of criminal law as a last resort relate to its application? Often, the more generally formulated penal provisions suffice; these have served well since the introduction of the Criminal Code in 1886 and have often proven capable of evolving with the times. These questions are justified, for as the House, we must critically examine legislation, and criminal legislation in particular. A particular aspect naturally at play in this bill—which we also heard just now in fine debates between colleagues—is that of conflicting fundamental rights. This calls for a respectful balancing of interests, and in that regard, it has indeed taken some time for this bill, which has been in preparation for several years. I also cannot view this in isolation from the complex trade-offs that the initiators have had to make. In that case, I look at the age limit, but also, for instance, at the restriction to the exercise of an office, a profession, or a business, or within the context of an organization. However, I also look at the serious manner in which they have weighed the advice of the Council of State and have amended and supplemented the bill and the explanatory memorandum. Mr. Chairman. Previous speakers have already described in a poignant way that conversion therapy is a serious problem, as the professional association of psychologists writes to the House: conversion therapy runs counter to the ethical standards of our profession, and not a single reliable study demonstrates that conversion therapy can change a person's sexual orientation or gender identity. What is certain, however, is that people who have undergone such acts can suffer serious harm, mental and/or physical: PTSD, feelings of guilt, depressive symptoms, anxieties, intimacy problems, and suicidal thoughts. The 113 Suicide Prevention Foundation also wrote a letter to the House just this week. I also cite the scientific article cited therein: conversion therapies significantly increase the risk of suicidality—thoughts, plans, attempts—for LGBTQ and transgender persons. This is no light matter, Chair! It should therefore be clear that D66 is positively disposed toward this bill. There is much public support, and the professional association of psychologists, as well as that of psychiatrists, 113 Suicide Prevention, and the LKP Foundation, have responded positively to the House. At the same time, a serious examination also calls for a number of questions—questions regarding the choices made and the reasons why this proposal is ultimately necessary. As I said, the first legitimate reflex is to be skeptical about new criminal provisions. After all, it is often the case that more broadly formulated articles would actually have sufficed, that phenomena that seem new ultimately fit within them, or that phenomena deemed punishable only later can still be covered by them. The initiators have pointed this out and have also argued why, in their view, existing legislation is insufficient in this case. The question has already been asked a number of times, but it seems important to me that this is addressed in more detail in the initiators' response. Where exactly is that added value? I would like that to be broken down for both the under-18 category and the over-18 category. But after the interruption debate at the very beginning of the evening — or was it the afternoon? — with Ms. Bikker regarding what the lower limit actually is for pastoral conversations, I would also like to hear from the initiators... The initiators do not want to touch that either. As far as I am concerned, there are certainly safeguards in the article-by-article explanation, but I agree with those who say: it is important to clarify that properly one more time. I say that while electroshock against someone's will is, it seems to me, already a punishable offense. So: what exactly is that gap that this fills? That this be clearly set out once again! In its advisory opinion, the Council of State indicates that the UN Committee on Human Rights and the Committee on the Elimination of Discrimination against Women have stated that "conversion treatments are discriminatory" and call for a ban on such treatments. The European Parliament has also stated: Member States, ban conversion therapy. This raises the question of what the initiators think of the current situation. Are we acting in violation of this? The initiators also bore in mind that they could have opted for the ultimate remedy character of a complaint-based offense. They did not do so, and it seems good to me that they say something about that. Regarding enforceability—as I also stated in the interruption debate—I derive a great deal of comfort from the fact that the Public Prosecution Service stated in its advisory opinion that it is "formulated sufficiently concretely to provide practitioners with tools for enforcement, investigation, and prosecution." Anyone who has frequently participated in debates on criminal law knows that the Public Prosecution Service does not always say that, and not without good reason. Then, two more questions regarding the legislative drafting technique. One. Something is indeed said about conditional intent in the explanatory memorandum — Ms. Wijen-Nass already touched upon it briefly. Could they elaborate on that, especially for the comfort of Mr. Diederik van Dijk? He stated, after all, that one does not want a chilling effect to occur due to a hint of conditional intent—loosely translated to what Mr. Van Dijk said—for matters that the initiators themselves believe should simply be allowed to take place. Finally. We recently discussed the Timmermans/Bikker bill here regarding discriminatory intent. A ground for increasing penalties was proposed there.
