In contrast, this proposal actually chooses to introduce a qualified variant in a separate paragraph, namely when it takes place in association, as a habit, etc. I would like to hear a bit more about the choice to structure it in this way. Thank you.

Yes, I will indicate when the time comes. Then a question from Ms. Jansen regarding enforcement in schools: can the State Secretary indicate what structural resources schools receive for this, how enforcement handles this, and whether the implementation is left to the schools? The latter is indeed the case. It is therefore left to the schools how they handle this. There is indeed a smoking ban in and around schools. Incidentally, this applies not only to smoking but also to vaping. They must establish, designate, and enforce this themselves. They are best placed to determine how to do this. They pay for this from regular funds; they do not receive any extra money for it. That is the answer to that question. Then the question from Ms. Slagt-Tichelman regarding supervision and enforcement in schools, since the previous question was about the money. Compliance is relatively high; we see that. The NVWA primarily conducts supervision based on risks and reports. So, it simply assesses where it needs to go in a risk-driven manner. That is the enforcement regarding schools. For potential illegal points of sale, for example, we use test buyers. The NVWA can get to work on that using the additional resources we are making available for that purpose. Then, regarding the question of the status of the preparation of the legislative proposal for a broader smoking ban in places frequented by children. That was a question from Mr. Krul. It is true that we are currently working on a legislative proposal to introduce a smoking ban at managed playgrounds, childcare facilities, and petting zoos. We are in discussions regarding this with various parties, as well as with the Ministry of Social Affairs and the State Secretary for Social Affairs. I hope to put that legislative proposal out for online consultation before the summer. Finally, regarding the question from Ms. Slagt-Tichelman about how I can reconcile examining with the Trimbos Institute how the budget cuts will be implemented, while simultaneously stating myself that the Helder op School program can continue next year. Funding for it will simply remain available next year, as those resources will continue until the end of next year in any case. After that, following 2026, resources will also still be available for the Helder op School program. Project funding is available for that. Chair, those were the answers to those questions. With that, I have come to the end of the section on youth and the school environment.

I agree that the current system is fragmented on this point. I also spoke of dispersed responsibility rather than clearly assigned responsibility. In the current system, this is unsatisfactory. That is also why we are currently standing here with a certain degree of embarrassment regarding this specific point. So I agree that precisely from the dimension outlined—that this does involve government intervention and is a government task—the system we are currently in is actually difficult to justify… I am looking for another word. It is difficult to explain as justified from that perspective. It is certainly part of the discussion regarding the decentralization of powers, but I think that we, at least from the Cabinet, recognize that in a new system there must also be more centralized possibilities and powers to intervene on certain points. Whether that should lie with the central government is a further question, because it naturally requires a degree of expertise. It does not make much sense to delegate a power to someone who lacks the expertise to pass judgment on it. However, I readily endorse the view that things must change in the context of government intervention in people's lives—young people, but also their parents.

As far as I am concerned, I have two answers to that. One answer is that the Russian Federation is currently an unimaginably strong war economy and is producing exclusively for military purposes at a very rapid pace. That is also the only reason why the economy is still functioning somewhat. This involves unimaginable sums of money, and it also involves unimaginable quantities of equipment and technology that will ultimately become available to the Russian Federation. The Russian Federation has also made it clear that it will not shy away from starting a war of aggression in Ukraine. That is the first answer. We must, after all, realize that we could end up in that situation. We must do everything possible to prevent that. Having a strong defense is, incidentally, also part of that. We are all convinced that the discussions from America towards Europe—which, incidentally, have been ongoing for years, and in which America states in various ways—and under the current administration even more strongly than before—that it wishes to fulfill its leading role regarding the protection of Europe within NATO differently and that Europe must do much more itself—are an additional reason why Europe must now ensure that it gets its own defense in order and spends more on defense. These discussions have, moreover, been ongoing for years. That is why it is also important that initiatives of this kind come from the European Commission to ensure that we can actually embark on that path together. I do not have a crystal ball, but it is clear to me that at the NATO summit, undoubtedly also under pressure from America, a robust discussion will be held regarding increasing the percentage. Any initiative that can help countries start this process or enter into such stages can provide support in ensuring that we can fulfill our defense obligations, and thereby ensure the security of Europe through our deterrence.

The Cabinet takes both the Framework Agreement and the coalition programme seriously. Those lines are perfectly clear. Today I have tried to indicate factually how we intend to handle this with regard to Ukraine. The fact that one of the parliamentary groups states that they also very explicitly want to include other subjects in this is something I will see during the discussions on the Spring Budget Memorandum. We will have that conversation then. I would also not be doing the debate justice now if we were to philosophize extensively with each other about what that will mean, precisely at the moment when we can properly continue the support for Ukraine, which was ultimately the subject of this discussion.

I find it difficult to make this commitment to Mr. Omtzigt, to be honest, because we have not yet been able to discuss this broadly within the cabinet. If Mr. Omtzigt asks me how the cabinet intends to deal with the region and with a greater presence of ministers and state secretaries in the region, I am happy to promise him that, but I back off at the words "institutional design." However, I am willing to enter into a debate with Mr. Omtzigt regarding a cabinet perspective on how we can involve the region more in our policy and regarding our presence in the region.

I think that the Minister of Defence will also say a number of things shortly regarding the European defence industry and developments. I think that we can actually return to this at an opportune time, as Ms. Bikker requests. I see the Minister of Defence nodding. I find that encouraging.

That realization was present not only from September, but even as early as July 2. In that sense, all discussions, starting from the introductory talks, have been guided by the understanding that this cabinet and the supporting parties do not have a majority for this in the Senate. The intensity has increased significantly over the past few weeks, as is appropriate for a budget debate and the democratic process of amendment. I subsequently defended my budget here in your House, and amendments were submitted. There was much discussion about this. Much support was also provided for various funding proposals. And as the end approaches, you also notice that the intensity increases; that is all part of politics. It is approaching its culmination this evening during the votes. Then it is up to your House. That is also how the democratic process is supposed to proceed.

I believe it was already becoming somewhat clear from the field. But if Mr. Jetten says, "VVD, now I am going to contribute ideas based on the political reality we find ourselves in, where things are actually possible," then I would be very happy to. I have ideas for that myself as well. But I am simply here in a political partnership. We made these agreements. Just as I was good for the agreements I made with D66, that is also the case in this coalition.

I must seek official advice on that matter. I do not know when the investigation will be completed. I do not know what will happen next. I do know that the investigation has been initiated. Again, that falls outside my direct portfolio. At this moment, officials are still working on determining whether this constitutes a case of unwanted interference. At the same time, I would like to add that I understand the emotions of the Israeli Cabinet and the Israeli Minister regarding the events in Amsterdam, for they were extremely serious.

I do not believe that we are distancing ourselves from that advice of the committee. The committee also says: do not be under the illusion that you are steering towards a quota or a maximum number; realize that these are indications related to a certain maximum population growth you wish to pursue, and then indeed examine, per flow, how such a range can help. Well, I believe I am openly indicating that I am genuinely willing to investigate that, certainly regarding labor migration, but the condition is that that number must also be of some use to you in terms of policy. It must at least be an indication of what you intend to steer towards; it must express a need within a sector to admit a certain number of migrant workers, for example, or a maximum or minimum number, but you must subsequently be able to do something with it. I will come back to this in a moment, but naturally—and a number of questions have been asked about this—we are facing limited possibilities within Europe to steer towards individual numbers, at least in the short term.

Yes, I would be happy to reflect on that. After all, that also justifies a substantive discussion regarding whether that is more useful for one type of migration than for another. With asylum, it is simply the case that you have less control over it, because the situation in countries can suddenly give rise to a substantial influx of asylum migrants. I refer briefly to the situation in Ukraine or the situation in Syria, which at a certain point led to us having to process large numbers of asylum migrants here. You could say: yes, okay, that falls outside or within the bandwidth. But the question is whether that provides you with a means of steering. That is simply the open question I am posing here. That is actually also what the committee itself says, namely that it will be more useful for one type than for the other. Regarding labor migration, I am happy to have that discussion with you. I will come back to this later in another motion, but I also think that if we are really going to have a discussion about what the economy of the future looks like... In which sectors do you think you will or will not need migrant workers in the longer term? Could you perhaps also quantify that need, so that you get a sort of target figure that you could do something with together with the business community? Well, perhaps such a range could then also have a guiding effect. I do think that the answer to the question of whether it can have a guiding effect is decisive for the answer to the question of whether or not you would do something with it. Again, it is not about the total range, because we all believe that you will have to get a grip on migration to arrive at that moderate growth scenario. Within that framework, you must be able to have the right discussion with each other.

I believe that throughout this debate I have repeatedly tried to indicate how important I consider volunteers to be for the Netherlands, and that the decision taken by the Cabinet was determined partly by this. In doing so, I have tried to dispel throughout the entire debate the notion that it was actually about five volunteers. Precisely because we, as a Cabinet, decided to award this, I believe I have truly demonstrated that this Cabinet stands for volunteers and for the decorations system as such.

The VVD's position is clear. I also hope that Mr. Bontenbal demonstrates to his European family that things can be done differently, and that one can also take responsibility in one's own country without incurring joint debts and creating new problems. But when you say "with a broad mandate," you are saying: we will look at it step by step. It is clear where the VVD stands on this, and where the CDA stands on it. Cabinet, enter into those discussions. It will also concern the question of what "peace" actually means; so we are still in the phase of these kinds of fundamental conversations. What does that look like? With whose support? How do you structure that? It therefore concerns all those issues that have come up recently. Then you return to the Cabinet and to the House with: these are the scenarios and this is what we would like to do. The VVD's position on this is clear. That is why I also say: step by step. I would not want to go in any particular direction now, except that I have said that I consider it important that every country takes its own responsibility.

Mr. Bontenbal and I have no difference of opinion regarding the importance of the decoration system, not as a system or structure, but because of the importance and appreciation extended to people who do beautiful and wonderful work in our society. That is ultimately the most important thing. I do not know if you consider it important, but some people, very many people, find it incredible to receive appreciation in this way for what they do and for the commitment they make to society. I felt it was important to ensure that this commitment was clarified, also more generally, within the broader context of volunteer work and the system. I could have chosen a different procedure until Sunday, but not after Sunday. On Sunday, I said: we are going to do this differently; the Chapter's request can be forwarded to the other ministers. With that, I believe I have set a standard. We may disagree on that, but I think I have made it clear that I stand for that system, in the interest of all those people who attach incredible value to being rewarded in this way. With this, we as the Cabinet have also been able to make clear how much value we attach to the volunteers who do that work in society. Within the Cabinet, there is simply the possibility for a minister to make an individual assessment, and the regulations also provide an option to consider how to handle that. But once again, normally this would have remained within the Cabinet, and you would never have heard anything about it here. In different circumstances, this would simply have been arranged in this way without anyone knowing.

First of all, I reiterate that people with dual passports have nothing to fear, except if they engage in terrorism. Moreover, that terrorism still needs to be prosecuted by the Public Prosecution Service, and a judicial ruling must be made regarding it. Therefore, there is no sword of Damocles hanging over people living here in the Netherlands with a dual passport. It simply isn't there. After all, you don't engage in terrorism just like that. It is not a vague boundary. It is not the case that you are deported for shoplifting. This is not about that at all, not at all. It is about terrorism. Only then can Dutch citizenship be revoked. We are looking into that, because it is conceivable within the current procedure. But again, the Public Prosecution Service must ultimately pronounce judgment on that. In the framework agreement, we have already indicated a number of avenues for how we intend to act on this. I therefore think that goes together perfectly, because if we combat antisemitism together in the Netherlands, regardless of your philosophical or cultural background, regardless of where your parents or grandparents come from, then the other might possibly not even be necessary. But it is good to have that stick to fall back on to ensure that you can also take tough measures, in addition to the fact that you are of course trying to resolve matters together here in the Netherlands.

Chair. The coming month is an intense sprint towards the NATO summit. The Netherlands has provided the Secretary-General of NATO. In recent years, we have demonstrated leadership in Europe by being one of the first countries to provide large-scale support to Ukraine. In that regard, we are still in the front group. We have also moved towards the 2% for defense. However, the decision in the Spring Budget Memorandum means that the Netherlands is now almost being overtaken by Belgium in terms of defense spending and that we are lagging behind Germany, Poland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Finland, Norway, Sweden, and the United Kingdom—all those countries we look up to. As far as we are concerned, it is necessary for the Netherlands to demonstrate leadership at the NATO summit. Hence the following motion, to encourage the Cabinet to demonstrate this as well. Let it also be a sign of support from the opposition for this important step. The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that the Secretary-General proposes to move towards a NATO standard of 5% by 2032, of which 3.5% is spent on defense; whereas spending 2% on defence is insufficient to keep the Netherlands safe and both the Cabinet and the House have already expressed their support for this; whereas, as host country of the NATO summit, the Netherlands has a special responsibility in shaping the discussion among allies regarding raising the NATO standard; requests the Cabinet to commit to the proposal of the NATO Secretary General and to convey this position at all upcoming ministerial meetings, and proceeds to the order of the day.

We agree on that to some extent, because in my view, there is already a certain industry in the Netherlands, industrial clusters with infrastructure, and a great deal of business activity. Such a cluster is a very fragile entity. If you pull a few cards out of it, the entire cluster could collapse, and perhaps nothing will come back. However, if, during the transition to sustainability, a number of companies decide not to invest—provided they have the preconditions in order—and they are replaced by another company in that location, thereby keeping the cluster very strong, then that is excellent. My point is that I do not want us, as the government, to steer at the company and sector levels, saying things like: this yes and that no. We will ensure the preconditions for a successful Dutch energy-intensive industry, because I also believe that the Netherlands is extremely well-positioned to sustain that energy-intensive industry for the coming decades as well, given our location next to the North Sea, the investments in nuclear energy, and so on. In my opinion, the Netherlands is a very good place to maintain a large basic industry in the future as well.

Then Ms. Michon-Derkzen had another question. I think it was actually about the same thing, namely self-direction, but it was essentially within the same framework. She wanted to hear from me that the neighborhood is safe, that people have been helped, and that I am committed to firmer intervention by the mayor. I understand that to be the intention behind this question. Mr. Six Dijkstra's question was actually about that as well, namely the need for earlier intervention by mayors in a less far-reaching manner. Here too, that hesitation to act is mentioned. Mr. Six Dijkstra asks me whether national guidance would help in this regard and whether I am willing to pay attention to it. The answer is yes — as I have just indicated — but you also ask me to what extent the existing guidance from the CCV is considered adequate and where there are still gaps. The CCV, the Center for Crime Prevention and Safety, has a large number of examples on its website of behavioral directives based on extensive case studies, which can therefore serve as guidance for municipalities and mayors. These behavioral directives can therefore also be imposed on people with confused and misunderstood behavior, but I would like to mention here that it is important that, if such an approach is adopted, it is done with the right combination of support and enforcement, as Ms. Steverink of the CCV indicated last year during the roundtable discussion. So not one and not the other, but a combination of support and enforcement. That means: collaborating with care services from the outset, jointly determining what a person causing a nuisance needs, and assessing whether a behavioral directive with a support component could contribute to stopping the nuisance. Finally, Ms. Wijen-Nass asked — this is the last question in this section — for mayors to take the lead and for municipalities, together with their care professionals and local police, to shape it themselves, because they know what is needed locally. She is curious whether the Minister is willing to do so. Let me start by stating that it is of primary importance that municipalities, care professionals, and the local police bear joint responsibility themselves, each acting from their own role and powers; that it is also relevant in the first instance that appropriate care and support are provided within the social and care domains; and that mayors therefore have a role and responsibility in extreme cases. Without creating new roles and responsibilities for mayors, I will therefore examine to what extent mayors can be offered more scope for action within their existing roles and powers. Then I come to the final block of questions, regarding housing. I am answering these on behalf of my colleague from the Ministry of Housing, Spatial Planning and the Environment (VRO), Ms. Keijzer. Ms. Mutluer's question was whether this subject is of interest to the Minister of VRO and whether the others will hold her accountable. The Minister of VRO has also committed to the joint approach. As you also state, she is indispensable for the joint approach. Unfortunately, it was simply not possible for her to be here this afternoon.

In my view, denying what took place there and which specific group was involved makes no sense. I think it was necessary to state that. I have not made any generalizing statements. I have made specific statements regarding the actions of young people. If you see the footage and hear what they are saying to each other and what is being shouted, it is clear that they are young people with a migration background. In doing so, I do not think I am making any assumptions. But otherwise, it is correct: naturally, all the facts still need to be investigated. These are of the utmost importance for the prosecution of this group of perpetrators. For let one thing be clear: it started with actions. It did not start with thoughts or words. It started with actions. Antisemitic violence was committed in Amsterdam in the year 2024. A disgrace to the country! Shame! That shame also crept over me on Friday in the European Council. You think: that this is happening in the Netherlands! That means we must take firm action against that group. There is no room for compromise there.
Here, it is specifically linked to this European Council next week, while the international discussion, including that within NATO, is still ongoing. That is why I consider it untimely.

Thank you, Chair. The House, having heard the deliberations, whereas evaluations have been published regarding tax schemes for entrepreneurs and the reduced corporate income tax rate; whereas the schemes leave room for improvement in effectiveness and efficiency, but the objectives of these schemes for SMEs remain valid, namely stimulating capital investment, innovation and employment, and mitigating the disadvantage suffered by SMEs compared to large companies in the areas of financing, competition and regulation; requests the Government, in consultation with (representatives of) SME sectors, to investigate how the objectives of the schemes can be better achieved, within or outside the schemes concerned, and to map out options for this purpose before Budget Day 2025, and proceeds to the order of the day.

In the coalition programme, funds have been allocated for additional legal aid and the improvement of primary care services. That is a response to this report. It is a step towards improving legal protection in the Netherlands. I already stated that it is more than that. Legal protection, social legal aid, and subsidized legal assistance require more attention and a more structural reorientation regarding the vision for the future and the appropriate model for social legal aid. But it is a broader issue, as you rightly noted. After all, legal aid and legal assistance are also provided in other ways. All of this is part of the letter we are going to write in preparation for the committee debate, and you could view that letter as a further elaboration of this part of the cabinet's response to the report we are discussing today. Then I come to the section on administrative jurisdiction. Mr. Flach asked what steps have been taken regarding the Administrative Jurisdiction Division of the Council of State and how they themselves have reflected on this report. Both the Administrative Jurisdiction Division of the Council of State and the administrative judges have reflected on their role in the childcare allowance affair, and these reflections have led to reports containing recommendations and action points. These were subsequently taken up within the judiciary itself. Since then, administrative judges attach more weight to the legal protection of the individual and actively seek out facts, evidence, and the consequences of decisions. Decisions by administrative bodies are also subjected to a more thorough review against the principle of proportionality than before. Within the framework of consultation and adversarial proceedings, a dialogue process has also taken place between administrative judges and parents and children affected by the childcare allowance affair. This brings me to the autonomisation.

There is a valid concern in Ms. Koekkoek's argument. In my opinion, our policy in recent years has been focused too much on subsidization to a certain extent. At the same time, we see that investments are still not materializing, because private parties say: "I still lack investment certainty and I cannot make my business case work because I might not know how much subsidy I will receive, how the market will develop," and so on. Often, there is much more to getting that business case off the ground, that investment certainty, the market, and clear legislation and regulations. I share that point. That is why I asked my question about the energy investment deduction. We see that the return—the money the government spends on it—results in a private investment of, I believe, a factor of twelve compared to the deduction. That is considerably more than with many of the subsidy instruments we use. Looking at this broadly and globally seems sensible to me.

Thank you for giving Mr. Mohandis the opportunity to elaborate on this. I think we are pulling on the same side of the rope. What made it clear to me was the example of the teachers. We also have a teacher shortage, and therefore we need career changers. We cannot solve the teacher shortage with only the young people attending primary teacher training colleges and obtaining a primary teacher training diploma—a primary teacher training diploma, just as there is also a diploma in archival science. What we need are career changers coming from their profession, whether electrical engineering or nursing, who say: "I am 40 or 45 now, and I want to start training young people. Through an accelerated career change track, I am going to ensure that I acquire extra skills and master pedagogy and didactics, so that I have adequate knowledge and experience." Of course, all kinds of certificates and the like can be attached to this, but we specifically want a broadening of scope, so that people can also choose to practice the wonderful profession of archivist through the Lifelong Learning track. I think that the KVAN, which Mr. Mohandis mentioned, can play a very important role in this. The KVAN is working on professional profiles. These can be taken into account, specifically in determining which educational programs and which types of programs actually lead to relevant and adequate knowledge. I could even imagine—though then I would be taking the KVAN's place—that the KVAN works on a professional register in which you can enroll and register which diplomas, knowledge, and experience you possess. Then the KVAN can decide whether you are included in the register. The professional group can do all of this. This can also be used to demonstrate that adequate knowledge and experience are present. If the amendment regarding Article 6.3 is adjusted so that it states "possesses a relevant and adequate education," and we know that the KVAN is working on this and that it is demonstrable that someone has a relevant and adequate education, then it would be legally secured in that sense. In that case, I would very much like to give the professional association the opportunity to give their profession the solidity, for the future as well, that is also desired by them.

I am, however, very curious about the following. Suppose you do indeed have another major customization agreement. Then you will face a wide range of permit granting processes, because all these projects are very complex. Some of these major sustainability projects involve dozens or even more than a hundred permits. Could the Minister, together with the IenW colleague, return in her next letter regarding customization to how we ensure that a technical detail in permit 97 out of 100 does not derail the entire investment after an investment decision may have been made? It seems to me that it would be good if we considered this together upfront, to prevent these kinds of situations.

The starting points of our parties are indeed different. You belong to a confessional party. We are a liberal party. The starting point is therefore different, and the priorities are also different; that is why I am telling this story and you are telling that one. I actually think it is good that a nice compromise has been found regarding the Tax Plan. Now I am also looking at my colleague Van Eijk behind you. The Tax Plan specifically pays attention to single-income households. I believe that good cooperation is being set up precisely for that purpose. Once again, our liberal principle is that people prefer to arrange things themselves, and that we should then primarily look to government policy.

I personally advocated as a spokesperson for resources for PersVeilig, for protective equipment and instruments for journalists, and for freelancers. I never saw Ms. Ouwehand doing that. So, taking the measure is fine, but make sure you have also dedicated yourself to it recently. When it comes to police officers, make sure you were always there when they really needed us, Ms. Ouwehand. Chair. I will continue. I had already covered the substantive points. I wanted to add that all these plans are framed by our fundamental liberal values. I have certainly posted another tweet.

Finally. I think there is also a sense of urgency, because farmers are now literally and figuratively up to their necks in manure, and a very hard blow looms for many farmers at the end of the year. I am therefore happy to follow Mr. Bontenbal's lead. I am also curious to see what the Cabinet's response will be tomorrow. But if we want to develop this proposal further, I believe we must also ensure that those 5 billion are available very quickly. After all, we cannot wait a single day to offer farmers that perspective.

Yes, this lump sum. This law was passed a number of years ago, but fortunately we are in our right minds, I would say, and we are still debating this law, because it can have unpleasant consequences for participants and has also proven to be unworkable. We must also keep our eyes open regarding the Future Pensions Act. A study by Nibud established that 80% of people want to be able to choose between a fixed and a variable benefit, including during the transition. Here, too, public support is very important. It is actually very similar, although the Future Pensions Act represents an even greater change and has a much larger impact on someone's financial situation. If the VVD attaches so much importance to freedom of choice, then I hope Mr. Aartsen also realizes that he cannot avoid the fact that we must offer freedom of choice in the Future Pensions Act as well. But let us now listen to Mr. Van Kent.

That is, of course, a political judgment. Everyone has the right to pass judgment on that. I am not going to reflect on that, because I have a framework agreement that I am loyally implementing. With this, I have answered the questions regarding budgetary policy and all questions regarding the European Commission and the Stability and Growth Pact. I am just looking around to see if there are any interruptions. No. Then I will move on to the next topics: public finances and underspending. That seemed like a nice segue in this context. I already alluded to the fact that the Autumn Memorandum is a bit… I am trying to proceed at a faster pace than during the Financial Review. Back then, we spent a bit longer together here. It seemed fitting to start with Mr. Idsinga's question, because I believe it is also symbolic of the repetition of moves compared to the Financial Review. Mr. Idsinga used the platform, after all, to present a private member's memorandum. We held exactly this debate during the Financial Review, when Mr. Idsinga was, of course, on my side. At that time, we also proposed establishing an expert group, as we foresaw that this discussion was imminent. That expert group is now in place. It includes incredibly intelligent and independent figures. I am impressed by what they are producing and how deeply they are delving. I have also extensively informed the House regarding when we will present this to the House, so that the House will then have the opportunity, should there be a need for it, to enter into a discussion with that expert group. It does not seem appropriate to me to subsequently reopen the entire discussion of the Financial Review. I would, however, like to add one thing to that. I would like to prevent us from unintentionally fostering mistrust regarding the forecast and the budgetary policy we have; these are also highly regarded, both domestically and internationally. That was precisely what we tried to prevent during the Financial Review. It is a fact that actual figures and forecasts diverged significantly in recent years. Nevertheless, I would like to briefly indicate why that was the case. In the first place, this had to do with the exceptionally high inflation we experienced. That is a denominator effect, an incidental effect, so it ultimately falls outside the estimates. At least, inflation does have to decrease, but we will discuss inflation in detail later. In addition, there were many windfalls as a result of the strong economic recovery following the corona pandemic, which led to increased revenue. At the same time, there was significant underspending. The Cabinet and the House of Representatives have held a discussion on realistic budgeting. That has nothing to do with forecasting, but with budgeting. I consider that a political issue as well. We can record large figures in the books regarding our ambition, but we also know in advance that this leads to underspending. I do not consider that realistic budgeting. In that context, we have already stated in advance that we know that a portion of the funds will not be spent. To prevent all those windfalls from being spent again, we are going to book this in advance as part of realistic budgeting. That is, among other things, the cash shift that Mr. Flach pointed out. These are not tricks, but are precisely part of realistic budgeting. Finally, estimates are also examined. Budgets and estimates are often confused. An estimate primarily concerns the revenue side. We then check whether the revenue we estimate corresponds with the actual figures. In the Autumn Memorandum, this deviated by only 100 million. Given the size of our economy, that is very accurate and very impressive. At the same time, we have also seen significant outliers in the past. The expert group looks into that as well. The House is fully informed about this.

Chair. Today marks the fourth time I have the privilege of shedding light on the budget of the ministry now known as Agriculture, Fisheries, Food Security and Nature. The agriculture and nature portfolio still gets my blood flowing. I derive energy from conversations with enterprising farmers reflecting on where they will stand with their businesses in ten or twenty years. Or from site visits with forest rangers, who, drawing on their expertise, explain how they contribute to resilient nature in our country. And yet, during the preparation of this budget, something else was at play for me compared to previous years. I ask you, Chair, if you are familiar with the 1993 film Groundhog Day. In this classic, a grumpy weatherman reluctantly has to make a report in a small town about the American holiday of the same name. The only problem is that every day, every morning, on the same day, he wakes up in the same way. Chair, don't worry. There is no grumpiness or reluctance on my part, for it is a privilege to be allowed to stand here, time and again. Nevertheless, I can well imagine that people following this debate are wondering when the agricultural and nature debate will finally move from words to deeds. We know that the concerns at farmers' kitchen tables are immense. That entrepreneurs are wondering what to do with their manure and that business successors, often very young people, are eager but waiting to take over the business. That is why I would like to hear from both ministers today what concrete actions we can expect to see from them in the coming six months. The graveyard is full of adopted parliamentary motions, both mine and those of many colleagues here in the House, to finally move on to, for example, targeted steering at the farm level. A concrete example is the system developed at AgroProeftuin de Peel for measuring and controlling nitrate leaching at the field level. Or consider the many innovations that can prevent nitrogen formation at the source while simultaneously providing fertilizer substitutes. There are plenty of examples. Whether we call it an accountable nutrient balance, MINAS 2.0, the secured Circular Economy Indicator, or a new term, when are we finally going to see this in practice? Is the Minister finally going to designate 25, 50, or 100 companies—just to name a few—in experimental areas where goal-oriented steering will simply be implemented in practice? I would appreciate a response.

Once again, I really hope for a slightly different attitude. Ours is constructive. Regarding pulling ahead: we are critical. We are not at all out to blame anyone. The only thing I am saying is that you must avoid being naive regarding these kinds of rules. You must prevent schemes from arising. Both the employer and, in this case, someone making use of it, or various human traffickers or other unsavory types, can abuse these. Let’s not be naive about that. I would also like to prevent, I say to Mr. Ceder, that we subsequently end up in a kind of game where we allow ourselves to be driven apart. In my opinion, this is precisely something we can work together on.

I am pleased to assure Mr. Dassen that I intend to look at the question in this way and also raise this with my colleagues in the European Council. The Minister of Foreign Affairs will also discuss this in his Council. Incidentally, the Minister of Finance will do the same in his Council. Then there was the question of whether the Netherlands can do more to locate and prosecute suspects of war crimes, particularly regarding Butcha and Irpin. The Netherlands supports Ukraine—Ukraine is, of course, the primary party—in this regard in various ways. This is done through capacity building at the Ukrainian Prosecutor's Office and among other actors in the judicial sector, through support for the International Criminal Court, and by working within a core group of countries on the establishment of a special tribunal for the crime of aggression. The Netherlands has offered, subject to conditions, to host the tribunals and is pushing hard in the negotiations for their rapid establishment. Mr Dassen asked about the European Sky Shield Initiative — I must pronounce it correctly — and he asked whether it can be deployed to keep Western Ukraine free of drones and missiles. As is known, the Netherlands supports any innovative way of examining how Ukraine can best be supported. I suspect that Ms Yeşilgöz has another question regarding the previous point, but you are in charge of the order, Chair, and not I, so I am looking at you for a moment.

In response to Ms. Bikker's question: I think that the Minister of Education, Culture and Science, and incidentally the State Secretary as well, need little to no encouragement to pay extra attention to this. They are both highly motivated. I noticed this during the discussion in the Council of Ministers last Monday. I can unequivocally promise that both ministers will get to work with the education sector to see how they can achieve this, so that there is more support for teachers, against all odds, to simply do this, because it has to be done.

As I just said: given the time and the four debates this evening, I went through it a bit faster. Leaving aside the intervention by Member Kostić for a moment: this would have been my answer if I had read it out in full. It concerns the following. I understand the rationale. We devoted intensive attention to this in the debate, specifically regarding emission data and the problems they pose concerning farmers' residential addresses. I indicated my personal view on the matter and stated that we really need to strike a balance. Based on the discussion we held during the debate, I am now giving the motion a judgment from the House. In doing so, I am also indicating that we, as a Cabinet, are truly prepared to enter into dialogue with other Member States to investigate the extent to which there is support there for further defining the scope of the term "emission data" in the Environmental Information Directives. My concern, therefore, is particularly with those residential addresses. I consider a residential address to be a truly unique type of data.

I would then like to mention a number of things regarding the requested measures. I would almost say that this is a preview of the plan we are expected to discuss in the Council of Ministers on Friday, regarding which I will say a few things and which the Minister of Justice and Security will elaborate on somewhat more. In general, this concerns a number of important matters, such as examining whether there are more possibilities in relation to the powers of the police under Article 3 of the Police Act regarding public order. But also, for example, the previously announced investigations regarding the ban on demonstrations, the ban on face-covering clothing, the use of expedited proceedings, and the discussion concerning Samidoun or other similar organizations. Incidentally, the private member's bill by Ms. Bikker and Mr. Timmermans regarding discrimination as a statutory aggravating circumstance also plays a role in this. With regard to a number of other topics, it also concerns increasing social safety in education and the Cabinet's intention to allocate funds to Jewish Social Work so that targeted aftercare can be provided to victims, or the structural funding of the Expertise Centre for Combating Discrimination within the police force, or the further implementation of the National Plan to Strengthen Holocaust Education. Thus, we have various aspects, both on the hard and soft sides, to consider how we can deal with these together. To Ms. Bikker, I can say that we will certainly also look—ultimately, I must add immediately, these are not very large amounts—at how foundations such as Kamp Westerbork and the National Committee 4 and 5 May, et cetera, can move forward financially and continue to devote attention to what I believe you call "Jewish life." Then Mr. Stoffer asked about the attention given to combating antisemitism and about the Holocaust during civic integration, and how this is reinforced. It is clear that it is necessary for everyone in the Netherlands to know that there is no place for antisemitism in the Netherlands. This also applies to civic integration course participants. The coalition programme states that civic integration must include knowledge of the Holocaust and its victims. The Cabinet is working on this and is pursuing it in various ways. Then there is Ms. Bikker's question: how is it possible that Judaism and the Holocaust are not taught in many schools in the Netherlands? Every school in the Netherlands is required to teach about Judaism and the Holocaust. The structural transfer of knowledge regarding Judaism and the Holocaust is embedded in the curriculum of both primary and secondary education. The Inspectorate monitors compliance with this. If such a school fails to teach about the Holocaust or Judaism, or teaches it insufficiently, the Inspectorate will take action against it.

Mr. Van Kent is now drawing a conclusion from this that I do not believe I drew during the debate. He relates it very directly to the threshold amount. Regarding the threshold amount, I indicated—which also brings me to the motion on document no. 660 by Mr. Flach—that the reasoning behind it is aimed at facilitating early retirement from the state pension and that we are mindful of the specific problems this creates with regard to the supplementary pension and the fact that you must continue to pay pension contributions and that you can also continue to contribute to that accrual. We will look into that explicitly, but he is now actually linking the discussion about the income requirement to this more than the discussion about the threshold amount. For that reason, I would certainly advise against it.

We certainly shouldn't be naive about that, but neither should we be naive regarding the question of how attractive the Netherlands actually is. Mr. Aartsen just touched upon a number of issues, such as bringing your family along. Well, that seems to me to be about the minimum to ensure that people think: the Netherlands might actually be an attractive country to work in. Could he then just briefly outline the whole spectrum and not just say that we need to close all doors? In my opinion, it also applies that we, as the Netherlands, must ensure that we remain attractive enough for those people we need so desperately.

I now turn to the motion on document no. 108. I can give the House my judgment on this. It concerns the retention of international students. I would like to advise against the motion on document no. 109. We also discussed this during the debate. We do not consider the Talent Pool to be a good measure. I can give the House my judgment on the motion on document 110, although I would like to clarify that we certainly do not deny that migrant workers make a valuable contribution to many sectors of the economy at this moment, but that we would also like to discuss with the House a more targeted and selective admission of migrant workers, and that this may also have consequences for the discussion on how we will view this flow of migrant workers in the future. With that clarification, I can give the House my judgment on the motion. Regarding the motion on document no. 111, I would like to say that it is untimely. Here I would like to make a commitment, namely that we will discuss Ms. Van der Plas's appeal within the Cabinet. We actually feel that this does not entirely belong in this debate, although I can also follow the reasoning as to why it might well be the case. However, a rural assessment for all legislative proposals and policy proposals is a very far-reaching measure. At the same time, it is an ambition of this Cabinet to take regions much more into account. Every region counts. We want to safeguard the balance between city and countryside. I would therefore like to ask the proposer, Ms. Van der Plas, for the opportunity to provide a response in which we indicate how we will proceed in the spirit of this motion. But at this moment, this motion goes too far for us.

Tweets will no longer be posted during the deliberations. In substance, this cabinet stands for the words I have spoken before: whether or not someone wears a headscarf makes no difference to the value we attach to every human being in this society. That is what the cabinet stands for, just as I stand with full confidence before all members of this cabinet.

I have stated that the term "undiminished" appears in the coalition programme. I am aware that the word "continue" appears in the framework agreement. For the Cabinet, "undiminished" is the line. Given the fact that the war is currently ongoing and that there is great pressure to ensure that we can continue to support Ukraine, I have stated that that series will be continued in 2026.

We share the view that this debate belongs here in Parliament. However, I also believe that the debate should be conducted in response to what is said here in Parliament. Therefore, in that regard, I refer to the debate with the Minister of the Interior. Subsequently, to the best of my knowledge, I have been exceptionally clear about how I support the mayor and how I believe the institution of the mayor should be supported, regardless of whether one should criticize it or not. However, I also stated that the right to demonstrate is rock solid. In my opinion, I have been clear regarding that within the context of the debate we are having here in Parliament. I hope that this debate, including the debate with the PVV, will continue tomorrow during the discussion of the Minister of the Interior's budget, following the lines of the input provided in the debate. For that is the only true place where the debate should be held: here in Parliament, in response to contributions made here in Parliament, and not in response to statements made outside this domain. That is not the case here.

That is the motion by Ms. Patijn. I must advise against it. That is not so much about back payments, but about a non-mandatory allowance, which is also tax-free. I must advise against that. I must also advise against the motion regarding document no. 778. We have held an extensive discussion on that. That concerns recovery. I can leave the motion regarding document no. 779 to the judgment of the House. That concerns the improvement plan and the application of the lessons learned from previous committees. The motion regarding document no. 780 concerns the call to interpret all relevant case law and regulations as much as possible in favor of the beneficiary. I can leave that to the judgment of the House, whereby we have explicitly agreed—also in a commitment to Mr. Vijlbrief—that we will ultimately shape that weighting in the recovery plan. The motion regarding document no. 781 concerns the recovery operations, namely taking 2006 as the starting year. I must advise against that. Regarding the recovery operation we are currently discussing, I have explained to you why 2020 is our starting point. It is also the case, I reiterate, that regarding indexation, for example, 2006 was indeed taken into account. Naturally, we look at the specific case and the issues involved each time. It is not the case that 2020 is the starting point by definition.

Last Tuesday, we voted on the motions regarding the state of public housing. One of those motions was the motion by colleague Vedder stating that parallel plans often get stuck in the pilot phase, whereas that should actually become the norm. We all stated in this House that we support that motion, because we believe that should become the norm. I do not need to touch what is already underway. I ask for attention to be paid to the matters that are not yet underway, which are tough and recalcitrant, and which truly require administrative courage to move forward. I have also heard a lot about local autonomy. Regarding that, I want to say that we must realize that, as the government, we are at the service of the citizen. We have local and regional autonomy, and that is power. Power must be legitimate. It concerns so many people in the Netherlands. Assuming two and a half people per household, over 400,000 homes correspond to one million people. If you assume that an average street in the Netherlands contains 25 homes, then everyone, on every street in the Netherlands, knows someone whose life is being put on hold by this housing crisis. At that moment, I say: something needs to be done about that.

Yes, I do indeed want to steer clear of technical matters for now. I agree with Mr. Grinwis that it radiates a certain degree of distrust. I believe that more figures and more controls are not always the solution. So, I have a substantive question. The tight labor market is one of the factors preventing a great many expenditures from being made. The question then is: what can still be done within the tax system and the benefits system to ensure that people actually work more hours? I am actually quite curious about NSC's solutions on that point.

No one benefits from having an image of a sector solely focused on the exploitation of migrant workers. In this regard, I would also like to add the following; and with that, I conclude my answer, Chair. It would, of course, be wonderful if people could be employed on a permanent basis. Actions are indeed being taken by companies to hire people on permanent contracts sooner. However, there are also many people who do not want permanent employment. Those people actually want flexible work. They actually want agency work. That opportunity must be provided as well. But let us please stop painting a picture that our companies in the Netherlands are out to exploit migrant workers.

The MFF is being discussed by the Commission at the European Council in a more communicative manner. It explains how it views the steps towards the 2027 process. All countries still have to determine their commitments in the letters of commitment. I think that virtually no country — and this certainly applies to the Netherlands — has yet formally ratified its commitment in the Cabinet, let alone discussed it in Parliament, as must happen in our case. The discussion in the Council this coming Thursday will therefore be extraordinarily cautious. But Mr. Omtzigt's words carry weight. I think I can certainly express the sentiment that we do not want taxes.

Chair, thank you. First of all, thanks to the Members of Parliament for the get-well wishes to the Prime Minister, which I have naturally passed on. I hope he will be back on his feet so that he can indeed attend this informal European Council—for that is how I interpret it—in person on February 3rd. It concerns, in English, a retreat—I translate that as a strategy day—of the members of the European Council, the heads of state and government who normally form part of the European Council. As you may know, a meeting with the British Prime Minister and a meeting with the NATO Secretary General are also scheduled on this occasion. I think that is sensible in order to project a unity in this regard that extends beyond just the EU and the EU Member States, and to exchange views there. It concerns an informal European summit, which will subsequently set lines of discussion that the Commission can use to its advantage when it releases the defence strategy in March. In June, via the General Affairs Council, this will be revisited at the European Council. But it is clearly a kind of strategic retreat taking place, with—I must add—the advantages, but also the disadvantages. There will be no joint communiqué. Nor will there be Council conclusions, as is normally the case. The disadvantage of this is therefore that, after the conclusion of that European Council, every head of government will go to the press with perhaps a slightly different or their own story regarding what they contributed at that retreat. Mr. Chairman. The current threat landscape and geopolitical developments call for a decisive approach in the field of security and defence. We have also discussed this in this debate. It is clear that the United States expects European countries to contribute more to collective deterrence and defence through NATO. We have discussed that as well. NATO, I also say to Mr. Diederik van Dijk, serves as the cornerstone of our collective security. I consider it important that we continue to cooperate closely with partners such as the US and the UK. The current security situation demands that countries make their contribution and, in any case, take the 2% agreement seriously. Continued support for Ukraine is also crucial. Numerous European countries could contribute better and more to this. It is important to conduct the discussion on security and defence within the EU in the proper order, I say in response to the House's first term. The Netherlands advocates, first and foremost, for a thorough analysis of the role and level of ambition of the European Union in relation to the member states and NATO. It must be clear who is responsible for what. The core question here is: what must the EU do to ensure that the European NATO member states possess sufficient military capabilities and can thus make an adequate contribution to those NATO objectives to safeguard our security? This requires a shared understanding of the joint capabilities that EU member states must develop and of how the EU can contribute to this through strengthened industrial policy and stimulating investment in defence production. In this regard, the starting point for the Netherlands is that we, as the EU, remain somewhat open to cooperation in partnerships with NATO and with countries outside the EU. The partnership with the US is naturally crucial in this respect, but I also mention the UK. For the Netherlands, the financing issue is the final piece of the discussion, where the primary focus must be on meeting the NATO standard. 23 of the 32 member states have now budgeted for that 2% target. Spending it remains a challenge, certainly after years of cutbacks and driving with the brakes on, but we are 23 of the 32 allies, so there are still a number of allies to go to truly reach that 2% in the budgets. Among them are very serious partners such as Canada, Belgium, Italy, Spain, and the like. That is an important point in the first place. More can also be done to mobilize private investment in the defense industry. I will return to that shortly. I should add: the Netherlands is emphatically not in favor of incurring common debt. The Prime Minister will also convey this during the informal European Council. Based on the analysis of the necessary industrial policy and capabilities, an estimate of the required financing will have to follow. A starting point here is that all those NATO member states structurally meet that 2% target. That will create a great deal of additional funding. I believe Mr. Draghi's report already stated that if all those member states were truly at that 2% spending target, it would ensure that an additional 60 billion euros per year could be added. Moreover, the defense industry also requires long-term certainty, which can be provided through such a structural increase in defense spending. For additional funding, the first step should be to look at how private financing can be stimulated. Banks, insurers, pension funds, as well as other private investors must be mobilized to invest more in the defense industry. Mr. Chairman, defense is something different from cigarettes. Defense, the defense of our freedom, must not be a taboo. Barriers must be removed in this regard. I consider it positive that the European Investment Bank has already broadened its lending mandate—a further broadening to core defense—while maintaining a triple A rating. The financial market access of the European Investment Bank must also be taken into consideration in this context. In my experience, it is therefore very important to enter into dialogue with the credit rating agencies. If it is clear that the shareholders of the European Investment Bank truly support that policy and consider it genuinely necessary and useful, then this could lead to a positive rating, but it is not always very direct and simple.

Thank you, Chair. To avoid a sort of rotation circus here, I will present the motions that fall under my responsibility. This is therefore not entirely in the order in which they were submitted, for which I ask your forgiveness. Let me begin by responding to Mr. Van Nispen's question. We will ultimately present a concrete elaboration of the targets around the time of the Spring Memorandum process. I have heard Mr. Van Nispen. I agree with him that it is not the intention for any part of those targets to fall within that implementation phase. I do not yet want to give a 100% guarantee that I will not look at the implementing organizations where it does not touch upon the implementation itself, but upon the organizations in a broader sense. That is simply because I cannot rule that out at this moment. I understand him well and will take the motion to heart with that in mind during the further process.

I hear Ms. Bikker saying that she is in favor of a ban on face-covering clothing at demonstrations. A motion regarding this was also submitted yesterday. We voted against it because we consider it entirely possible that there are various situations in which this is justified, for example, if you wish to demonstrate against an unfree regime. I am referring to the Iranians in the Netherlands. They simply have things to fear. Their families have things to fear if the Iranian government sees that they are demonstrating. So there is a reason that such freedoms exist. I understand that they can sometimes be misused, for example, when people abuse a demonstration for intimidation, as Ms. Bikker rightly points out. I agree with that. But that is why we have vested that discretionary power in the municipality. Municipalities must assess in which situations demonstrating with face-covering clothing would and would not be permitted. If you introduce a nationwide ban, which I understand Ms. Bikker is in favor of, then you lose that leeway. In the name of safety, a great many people lose freedoms that can indeed be useful in a free society.

Yes. Indeed, Chair. I can combine both of them in one and the same memorandum. Ms. Wijen-Nass also asked why the sentence has been increased to six months. It has indeed increased significantly, but the nature of the offense has also changed, from a minor infraction to a felony, from one month's detention to six months' imprisonment. I believe that this increase aligns better with other electoral offenses, for example with bribery, obtaining a proxy through bribery. I also believe that this increase does much better justice to the seriousness of the offense, because soliciting is simply a serious offense, a criminal offense, and therefore, as far as I am concerned, also a felony. Ms. Wijen-Nass then also asks why the wording of Article L 2 was chosen. Article L 2 determines the manner in which a proxy may be granted, and it has now been explicitly added to the article that the initiative to issue that proxy must always lie with the grantor, the principal. In this way, it is also clarified that the choice of an authorized representative should lie with the voter themselves. Then I move on to the next section: feasibility. A question from Ms. Chakor. She asks whether we have sufficient insight, and whether the number of reported cases is representative of the whole. She asks if it might be the tip of the iceberg. I think that could indeed be the case. We have no indications that solicitation occurs regularly in the Netherlands. During the 2022 municipal elections, for example, the Public Prosecution Service proceeded to prosecution for solicitation in only one case. However, I do believe that every case of solicitation is one too many, because electoral fraud in any form truly undermines public trust in elections. With this law, we are also looking ahead and preparing for the possible and, to some extent, the unforeseen, certainly in this rapidly evolving digital era. We want to discourage solicitation in any case, and if it occurs, we want to tackle it more firmly. Ms. Chakor stated that political parties must be clearly informed regarding the granting of proxies and asked for a response. She also asked whether I have concrete examples of what is and is not permitted for political parties and whether I intend to issue guidelines. For the time being, I do not intend to issue new guidelines for political parties. The same principle applies to political parties as to all other parties involved in the election process, namely that a voter must be able to determine, without coercion or pressure, how and whether to exercise their right to vote. That freedom of voting is fundamental. Political parties must be fully aware of their role in the election process, just like anyone else who plays a role in it. Any initiative to vote by proxy must therefore always originate from the voter themselves and not from a political party. An offer of mediation by a political party to have a voter make contact if they are unable to vote themselves and, for example, do not know any other potential proxy, does not fall under the definition of soliciting, provided that the voter contacts the party themselves and states that they wish to appoint a proxy. I really expect political parties to handle this in a responsible manner, and at this moment I have no indications that there would be any problems with that.

Chairperson. The House, having heard the debate, noting that our country is currently facing significant shortages, not only in technical professions but also in other sectors; being of the opinion that aligning our education system as closely as possible with the labor market of today and that of the future is essential, so that future employees and employers continue to find each other; considering that aligning our education system as closely as possible with the labor market also benefits the economy; requests the Government, in cooperation with the Ministries of Education, Culture and Science (OCW), Social Affairs and Employment (SZW), and Economic Affairs (EZ), to conduct a labor market scan to clarify which occupational groups and thus study programs are necessary for our future earning capacity; requests the Government to ask the Ministry of OCW to analyze the results and to submit to the House various scenarios regarding what is needed to align our educational offerings with the labor market from a long-term perspective, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I think that one of the very positive aspects of the coronavirus recovery fund has been that investments were made on the one hand, and reforms were set up against them on the other. I think that would be a very good model to implement for the MFF as well, so that you can make those investments at the European level. That is not naive. That is precisely ensuring that we tackle the major challenges we face, which we cannot handle on our own, at the European level. Mr. Chairman. One more thing, while I see Mr. Omtzigt shaking his head very hard. This coalition, this cabinet, also wants a capital market union. We want that to ensure that we can invest in the future together. A good capital market union must also have a safe asset, because otherwise the capital will be gone in no time the moment something happens. Those safe assets are eurobonds. That is what every expert says, namely: make sure you do that together. From the very beginning of my time here, I have not understood the fear in this House. And that fear is always beautifully represented by Mr. Omtzigt.

I would like to take this suggestion to the European Council. In any case, the Middle East is on the European Council agenda. I assume that all Member States will support a ceasefire and that, at the same time, we will resume the discussion on how we can implement it more effectively in terms of material resources than in recent years.

I am pleased with this question. Against the backdrop of the deteriorating situation on the front and the poor situation regarding, in particular, the energy infrastructure for Ukrainian households—the people living in Ukraine—we have stated that it is necessary for us, in a European context or otherwise, to examine how we can do everything possible to ensure that the people in Ukraine get through this winter. That is one point. And two: with any military support we can provide to Ukraine—and this also applies, incidentally, to shifting from promising to delivering; much is promised, but not always delivered—we must truly ensure that Ukraine stands strong in the fight in the coming period and in the spring. That is what Mr. Brekelmans intended to indicate in so many words, that is what I want to indicate, and that is also my commitment in discussions within the European Union.

I have heard that appeal clearly. I hope to truly convince Mr. Krul, as well as my other colleagues in the House, that this is a subject of the highest priority for the Cabinet and for me as Minister. Therefore, we are not simply settling for that postponement. We are actively looking at how we can actually anticipate the implementation of the law, because as far as I am concerned, its coming is not up for debate. I repeat once again: we are also genuinely working with municipalities to see how we can enable them to fulfill their role and responsibility in this matter.

I was also pleased with the Minister's response. It was quite firm. I believe it is clear that everyone in the Netherlands enjoys protection. As far as I am concerned, it is important that we also discuss this in the committee debate in December. I do not know if the debate can take place sooner, so I would prefer it to be in a committee.

Thank you, Chair. The House, having heard the debate, noting that within NATO increasing emphasis is being placed on a more balanced distribution of responsibilities, whereby Europe must assume greater responsibility for the defence of its own territory; whereas it is important that European countries within the NATO alliance are better able to independently develop and maintain military capabilities; requests the Government, in a European context, including the United Kingdom and Norway, to urgently map out the critical military capabilities Europe needs to possess in order to be able to defend itself independently if necessary, and to report back to the House on the progress within two months, and proceeds to the order of the day.

I regret that. I am convinced that I consider any initiative that can contribute to ensuring that defense in Europe is strengthened to be worth examining. After all, we are standing at a turning point in history. That has already been stated by many today and in the previous debates we have held together. I consider the idea of pulling this off and seeing how we can ultimately do so under all the conditions we desire to have, to be simply a responsible course of action in the context of the security of Europe.

In the European context, it was important to give impetus to that way of thinking. That is why a creative look was taken at what might be possible, with all the discussion we have had about that here in this House last time and now. In the national context, I have already said, also last time — and I repeat that now — that as far as we are concerned, the discussion is being conducted along the lines of, once again, capabilities. Undoubtedly, the discussion will be addressed during the Spring Memorandum, at least with the coalition parties but also broadly in the House, to examine how the Defence budget can be increased. Is that feasible, in what way, and with what steps? That must be weighed against all the other priorities facing this Cabinet. I have not committed myself in any way, and will not commit myself tonight either, to any percentage whatsoever, whoever said it. Mr. Chairman. Mr. Omtzigt asked further…

Addressing Mr. Vermeer, I say: we can, of course, always improve the atmosphere by supporting each other during the votes. I also wish to ask the Minister a question in response to the interruption by colleague Van Eijk. Just now, I made an interruption regarding the process and stated that we must have in writing and clearly what we are agreeing upon, should the motion be adopted and we subsequently proceed to vote on the entire Tax Plan and everything associated with it. However, we must also pay attention to the substance. I sensed that briefly from Ms. Van Eijk, but I want to make that clear myself in any case. It is about more than that. The motion concerns an alternative implementation of the reduced VAT rates in the proposal. The Minister is correct that there are currently various amendments from colleagues before the House regarding sports and culture. There is an amendment from myself and colleague Stoffer concerning books, newspapers, and magazines. However, there is no amendment regarding accommodation. From that alone, one can already draw conclusions about what that alternative implementation might look like. However, I do not believe we should proceed by now limiting that alternative implementation to even fewer VAT categories. The motion speaks for itself. The amendments submitted by myself and various colleagues also speak for themselves. That should be sufficient for now, it seems to me, regarding the question of within which frameworks, which boundaries, we are seeking alternatives.

Thank you, Chair. Thanks also to the members for their input and motions. Before I proceed to the assessment of the motions, I would like to briefly respond to Member Bruyning and her requests. Prevention is very important in tackling undermining crime, especially when looking to the future and preventing a next generation of criminals from growing up. I therefore share that heartfelt wish, that heartfelt plea, and we will keep the House informed of our plans in that regard. I also share her concerns regarding the resilience of local government and the fact that administrators, as announced again today by the Mayor of Lochem, are declining positions in public administration due to threats from criminals. That is very concerning. You can rest assured that the Public Prosecution Service and the police will always act in such cases to protect local government. In addition, there is an ongoing program to increase the resilience of local government. This is led by my colleague from the Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations. A very important point, and thanks for the input on that. I will now move on to the motions. I will address them in the order in which they were submitted. I begin with Ms. Mutluer, with the motion on document no. 445, to advocate for a European ban on heavy fireworks. I leave the judgment on this motion to the House. You will be kept informed of any developments in this area through me or the Minister of Infrastructure and Water Management. Regarding Ms. Mutluer's second motion, the motion on document no. 446, I express my appreciation also on behalf of the State Secretary for Taxation and the Tax Administration, in whose domain this primarily lies. The Ministries of Finance and Justice and Security already held a discussion on this idea with the Dutch Association of Insurers and the Dutch Banking Association on September 24. The State Secretary for Taxation and the Tax Administration will explore this and will inform your House of the conclusion. With that, I also leave this motion to the House's judgment. Then, the Van Nispen motion on document no. 447 concerning companies conducting their own investigations and reporting themselves. The Public Prosecution Service's directive is intended to reduce class-based justice by ensuring that more investigations are conducted. The Public Prosecution Service remains responsible for the handling of criminal cases, but focuses more heavily on companies than on individuals. Incidentally, individuals are also permitted to conduct their own investigations. No distinction is made in this regard; rather, it is specifically intended to impose an additional hurdle on those companies. To that end, it might be beneficial for you to review the final content of that directive. I previously informed you that I expect the Public Prosecution Service to present a draft directive to me in October. I would like to share this with you at that time so that you can form an opinion on it. My request, therefore, would be to postpone the motion until that directive is available. Otherwise, I will have to advise against it.

In response to the first part of Mr. Van Kent's question, I state that we have discussed this before. I reiterate here that when people are victims of government errors, the guiding principle for this government, too, is that you do not become a victim of them. That is one point. Two. In the specific situation regarding the benchmark wage, I find it really important to first clearly identify the numbers involved and whether this problem actually occurs to this extent. The suggestion is being raised somewhat that a great many people might have just missed out on their benefit because they are or are not quite under 35, or that they did or did not receive an IVA benefit. Let us be careful not to exaggerate that problem prematurely, because I do not know if that is the case. I am well aware that Mr. Van Kent has submitted a motion in this Chamber. We must implement motions, so we will take that into account in that regard.

No. A few things are getting mixed up here. One. The IHRA definition is not a legally binding instrument and will not become one. The Public Prosecution Service is, of course, free to use that definition as a guideline or a helping hand to identify discriminatory aspects. As a cabinet, we have also established this in the anti-Semitism strategy. So it will not become legally binding. However, it can assist in the assessment of elements. What I said about legal anchoring concerns this legislative proposal. The statutory definition of the discriminatory aspect—not the IHRA definition, but the broader definition of the discriminatory aspect—will therefore be included in Section 44bis of the Criminal Code.
