That will soon become the guiding principle for criminal liability. I reiterate that this will therefore not become the IHRA definition as such.

Thank you, Chair. Thanks to the government ministers for their answers, their reflection on how the process nevertheless derailed, and their intention to keep it more on track from now on. I would like to echo what the Minister said: it is a matter of making choices in scarcity. At the same time, I notice essentially the same dynamic here and in the debate on the prison system. The coalition determines, via the Framework Agreement, what is left out—financial frameworks, financial choices, symbolic politics regarding harsher sentences, a ban on community service—but fails to deliver regarding the solutions that are subsequently needed and the choices that must be made, which are not always pleasant; then half or three-quarters simply refuse to cooperate. Little prospect is offered for a structural solution or improvement. Subsequently, to the other half, or less than half, of the House, a sort of "we are counting on your support" or "we hope for your support for this" is said. I think that is going to become a problem at some point. That flexibility will eventually run out. In my opinion, the solution lies in those structural solutions. I therefore also call upon these ministers to put more effort into this.

Chair. I would prefer a regular debate rather than an interpellation debate. However, I will not withhold support based on the format. The subject is too important for that, as the Israeli government must be held accountable. Therefore, I support the request.

What Mr Grinwis says is correct. He is essentially saying the following: the housing market is broader. Therefore, you should not look at just one law. Also look at taxation, including in Box 3. Your House is holding important debates on Box 3 with my State Secretary. I would like to propose including this in that discussion as well, because we are, of course, on the eve of a major reform in Box 3.

The suffering in the Gaza Strip does not escape me either; the suffering of the inhabitants of Gaza, of the Gazans, of the Palestinians, and also the suffering of the Israeli hostages, who could be released by Hamas today or tomorrow if Hamas so wishes. I have now also expressed my condemnation here in the House regarding the withholding of humanitarian aid and electricity, because in my view it violates international humanitarian law. That condemnation therefore stands. At the same time, everything must now be focused on what is currently most promising. At this moment, extending the ceasefire is the most promising option. I am discussing this with various foreign colleagues, including those from Qatar, for example, and countries that clearly have ties with Hamas, as I also did in Ankara the day before yesterday.

We are aware of such a report and such statements. For a description of the events in Amsterdam, I naturally refer to the factual account of the Municipality of Amsterdam and the response from the Minister of Justice and Security. Ms. Piri also already mentioned the letter from the Minister of the Interior and Kingdom Relations. We are studying to what extent there may be instances of unwanted foreign interference. If this is the case, we will act accordingly. We have not yet determined whether this is the case. Nor has any official advice been provided on this matter yet. In accordance with the letter from the Minister of the Interior, we do not currently see that specific intelligence has been shared. We have not yet been able to ascertain this. This is typically a subject that I would like to discuss further with the Israeli authorities.

That is correct. In that sense, it is a pleasant coincidence that I was able to make these remarks here today in this debate on the budget of AZ and the Royal House, partly in response to Mr. Klaver's contribution. I also felt it was important to do so. However, I am not reviewing the views of an individual party leader. I am giving support to the mayors who deserve it and I am standing up for the right to demonstrate. That is also my duty as Prime Minister.

My apologies. I share the view that there can be very different views of human nature, which are indeed represented in this coalition as well. Earlier this morning it was also stated that, as Prime Minister, I am the primus inter pares. This means that my view of human nature is not the guiding principle for the views of this cabinet. The guiding principle for the views of this cabinet is what is put forward by the four different parliamentary groups, or at least by the Framework Agreement. Together, we in the cabinet are trying to bridge this gap, drawing on undoubtedly many different backgrounds. I have also indicated—not here in your House, but at other times—that I consider it important, in my role as Prime Minister, to bring those different visions together and perhaps also to exercise a certain pragmatism in doing so. I intend to do this to ensure that we do not repeatedly get entangled in party-political discussions within the cabinet. I want us to look at where we can achieve results and where we can meet each other within the cabinet based on different perspectives, in order to take steps from there. We will be accountable for this in this House. We will do so transparently. You can see that in the Budget Memorandum and in the coalition programme.

This time indeed calls upon us to look at a great many points. That is also part of the subject of this debate. This has to do with European security, Ukrainian security, and Dutch security, but it also has to do with security guarantees, the peace negotiations, the peace agreement, and everything connected with them. At the same time, I think the following: we have always applied very strict criteria for accession to the European Union. We can examine whether the process can be accelerated. But if we abandon those criteria, we make things incredibly complicated for ourselves, also with regard to many other European countries. I admit: in a different context… But we are also holding many other talks with Ukraine to ensure that we can fulfill our responsibility towards Ukraine.

Mr. Vermeer had asked me a question about the refining route, but I believe it was just answered during an interruption. I am looking in Mr. Vermeer's direction for a moment. Yes. Then Mr. Grinwis asked me about the reservoir project... But Mr. Grinwis is not here at the moment. Mr. Flach is going to pass on the answer to him. Mr. Grinwis drew my attention to the Delta Programme 2021, a major energy storage project. I tell Mr. Grinwis that energy storage, as a form of flexibility, plays a very important role in our future energy system. Potentially, this project also offers a form of large-scale storage that can contribute to that flexibility. I am therefore prepared to map out the pros and cons in more detail together with the initiators, including a comparison with other flexibility applications. Mr. Chairman. Then I come to affordability.

I would like to say to Mr. Timmermans that the assessment made by the Minister of the Interior and myself differs from that of the Minister for Asylum and Migration. In our assessment, we very explicitly took into account the interests of volunteers in this country, regardless of their location, and the importance of the decorations system. We ultimately managed to convince the Cabinet of this as well. These two factors led the Minister of the Interior and me to the conclusion quite quickly that we had to proceed differently and ensure that the five volunteers involved would receive their decorations. We are now awaiting the submission of the nominations from the Chapter. This is a broader assessment than the professional assessment made by the Minister of Asylum and Migration. Subsequently, I discussed this with the Minister of Asylum and Migration and with the Deputy Prime Ministers to ascertain that the Cabinet supports this. The Cabinet does support it. For me, that brought things full circle, because it ensured unity of Cabinet policy and allowed the decorations to be awarded. With that, the discussion was closed as far as I was concerned.

There is an assumption in Mr. Dijk's question. That assumption is that the CDA would currently take all those cuts upon itself. Let me make one thing clear. I am not standing here to defend a compromise. What was bad is less bad. As far as we are concerned, that makes this Education Budget still strongly inadequate. What would I like to say to these organizations, whom I have actually already spoken to today? That they may expect from us that what we have done now will always remain our commitment, and that next year, during the budget discussions, we will again present constructive and sound, but also realistic, proposals to get those same cuts off the table.

Then there is the motion on document no. 22, also by Ms. Joseph, regarding the tightening of legal requirements for communication. I leave that to the judgment of the House. I must add, however, that the motion contains various observations and concerns regarding communication. I have already indicated that I am prepared to tighten the law regarding the unrealistically high expectations. Regarding a number of other points, I question whether that is desirable. But with this explanation, I can give the House my judgment on it. This means that I will address those specific points in the elaboration thereof. Then the motion on document no. 23 by Mr. Van Kent. That is a motion for speaking out, so I assume I do not need to give an opinion on that. The motion on document no. 24 by Mr. Ceder et al. challenges me to get to work quickly on the points I have mentioned and to bring them to the House. I can give the House my judgment on that.

I appreciate the urgency expressed by Ms. Kathmann in this debate. Broadly speaking, I can also follow that urgency. On the other hand, the proposals she makes are, in my view, very draconian. We cannot stop all migration today. We cannot eliminate the entire Microsoft Office package at once and suddenly offer our civil servants something else tomorrow. So I understand the urgency, but I would like to hear some more reflection and more depth from Ms. Kathmann regarding the timeliness with which she intends to submit these proposals.

Then we must also hold the debate with those coalition colleagues. I am standing here on behalf of the cabinet, of course, and I am not going to defend the plans of one specific coalition party. In a more analytical sense, I found the call to spend less and examine where the burden can be reduced to be a sensible plan. That will not surprise you. I think that is a recipe for combating inflation. Where to look for it is, of course, a separate matter. That is something we can debate with one another. We just had a debate on the revenue side. In addition, the choice of the type of expenditure you are going to reduce also affects inflation to a greater or lesser extent. That is correct. In the search for coverage, political priorities are naturally also taken into account. How do you view development cooperation, for example? That is really the debate that the parliamentary groups must hold among themselves. I try to remain neutral on that. I also sometimes use the word "agnostic." I try to remain agnostic on that, I say, when I am facing Mr. Grinwis. But let me make one point. Mr. Grinwis has drafted alternative budgets in the past, which often included tax increases. I think it is good when parties, from left to right, come up with proposals that are actually funded. I truly applaud that. This strengthens the debate. That is what I particularly appreciate. We must debate among ourselves how to flesh this out.

If it is not primarily about freedom of expression but about constituting a means of coercion, a coercive measure against the government or others, then it is not a demonstration. Finally, I have the motion regarding document no. 27 by Ms. Lahlah and Ms. Bikker. I must advise against it, because the WODC is independent and the government does not intend to add research questions to the study. But apart from that, the aim of the study is also to perpetuate the right to demonstrate. The study is expected to be completed in the summer of 2025. I have one more, Chair. The motion regarding document no. 32, by Mr. El Abassi, is superfluous. For the Cabinet already protects the right to demonstrate; you can truly rely on that. The WODC study safeguards the input of various civil society organizations, including human rights organizations. Moreover, those organizations are also part of the societal dialogue that the Minister of Justice and Security and I are conducting together. Therefore, this is superfluous.

Regarding resilience, I have stated that a plan is forthcoming. This affects critical infrastructure. It addresses many of the aspects mentioned by Mr. Timmermans. It concerns the manner in which we can build up that resilience. After all, in many cases, that resilience is still insufficient. However, all of this will cost money, and that will therefore raise all sorts of new questions on that point. Regarding the hard defense side, if I may put it that way, and the hard defense industry, cooperation is absolutely essential. The Dutch industry and the Dutch defense apparatus can play an incredibly important role in both. We are particularly strong in high-end technology. There are also a few specific manufacturing industries in which we are very strong. We are working in various ways to ensure that we align in various ways with everything currently happening around the defense industry in Europe and in the Netherlands. We do not want to be outmaneuvered by a few major players or a few major countries. That is indeed essential. With regard to capabilities, I have already indicated that this discussion will take place entirely within the domain of NATO in the coming months. Mr. Chairman. I will now quickly go through a few matters related to defense, about which we have already said the necessary details. I will skip those for convenience. However, there are, of course, a few things I still need to pay attention to. Mr. Vermeer asked how we prevent the intellectual property of the defense industry from falling into the wrong hands. In the Netherlands, we have the so-called Defense and Security-Related Industry Resilience Act. This includes a security screening mechanism. This investment screening mechanism safeguards national security regarding investments, mergers, and acquisitions within the defense industry. This Act will enter into force in the course of 2026. The abbreviation for this Act is "Vifo Act," I tell Mr. Vermeer. Then Mr. Dassen asked about a European intelligence service. First of all, the strategic decision-making of the European Union in the current global balance of power requires a strong intelligence position. You can rest assured that current geopolitical developments are receiving the special attention of the intelligence and security community. International cooperation between the services is of crucial importance for the protection of national and international security. The Netherlands is therefore already working on strengthening the existing EU intelligence analysis capacity, the so-called Single Intelligence Analysis Capacity (SIAC), in which this type of cooperation takes place. The Cabinet does not make public statements regarding the concrete implementation of these international multilateral cooperation frameworks, but you will undoubtedly understand that. The House is informed about this exclusively through the appropriate channels. Mr. Dassen also asked: how is the Prime Minister going to create public support? We have just had a discussion about that. I believe it is called strategic communication in official jargon, but it boils down to the fact that it is indeed important that the Cabinet, led by the Prime Minister but absolutely also supported by fellow ministers, ensures in the coming months and perhaps even years that we can properly explain to the Netherlands why the war in Ukraine is partly of direct importance to security in Europe and the Netherlands. We have already been working on this over the past few months, each in their own way. But it also concerns all the major choices we have to make together. Quite a few choices are coming our way. Incidentally, these are not merely financial in nature. Mr. Omtzigt already asked about this in the previous debate, using words to roughly this effect: how is the Prime Minister going to explain to the Netherlands that all this is necessary? I realize that very well, as do the ministers. We will and must address this in various ways. Mr. Chairman. I believe I am now finished regarding European defence. I will now move on to Gaza and the Middle East.

I will come to that in a moment, I say to Mr Aartsen. But I will start with the question from Mr Ceder, who asked me about the urgency of simplifying social security. I actually indicated this just now: I feel that urgency and I also attach great value to that simplification. I recently informed the House about my plans in the letter regarding the income support reform agenda and also drew attention to the letter that the State Secretaries for Finance and Allowances had sent earlier. In that letter, I indicated that I essentially place three things at the center of it: adequate income support; it must offer more security than it currently does and be easier to understand; and working must pay off more. I would like to work this out further together with your House, and with social partners, municipalities, and implementing bodies. But also with your House. That simplification will really require input from your House and the input of your parliamentary groups, but also the willingness to lay a common foundation for a future-proof system. I believe that we can ultimately only achieve this with a large parliamentary majority here, with a system that stands the test of time and truly lays the foundation for a future-proof reform. And of course, I would have presented that result here today—the sooner, the better—but it will also need to be done carefully. Our ambition is to determine that course together during this cabinet term. This involves revising the benefits and tax system, but also simplifying income support. And naturally, we will do what we can do in the short term. We will already be doing the things that anticipate this and fit within that context. I just briefly mentioned the legislative proposal on proactive service provision. But we are also working on the Balanced Participation Act, which my colleague will address shortly, and we are already solving problems such as the gross-net issue. In this way, we are also working step-by-step towards a number of reforms. Mr. Flach asks when the House will hear more about the simplified child benefit scheme with a single administrator. I informed you about the planning for this in mid-November. We aim for a letter containing several variants in the spring, and as far as I am concerned, that is also the starting point for entering into a dialogue with you on this matter. Further elaboration can then follow in the autumn, with a final letter containing a proposal in the spring of 2026. Ms. Patijn suggested that the mini-system is being introduced in my letter. I have just briefly responded to that. I think that is a misunderstanding, however, because the letter itself explicitly states: we are maintaining good insurance against loss of income due to unemployment, illness, or disability, with social assistance as a safety net. The ambition is truly to make that system simpler, more understandable, and more predictable. So, fewer regulations, less uncertainty, where people can assume that they receive what they are entitled to. Then there is Mr. Aartsen. He asked about the third objective of the reform agenda, namely that working more must pay off. In doing so, he also mentioned the aspect of the marginal tax rate. That is correct; the reform agenda indeed has that as an explicit objective. A lower marginal tax rate gives concrete substance to this, certainly when it comes to taxes and allowances. Thus, the agenda has three goals: that work must pay off, that it offers more security, and that it is easier and simpler to understand, taking the existing income distribution and budgetary frameworks as a starting point. Incidentally, I also explicitly indicated this in the letter. Under the principle of a secure and predictable income, it is explicitly stated: where possible, the system operates with fewer income-dependent schemes or with income-dependent schemes with a less steep phase-out. It is therefore also included as an explicit point of attention in the letter, albeit perhaps not under the name "marginal tax rate." After all, people must have enough left over from every euro earned. The uncertainty regarding the consequences for benefits when moving towards more work must be reduced. The marginal tax rate applies less to social security than to the tax system. That is also a large part of that agenda. That is why I speak more broadly about "more work must pay off" instead of limiting it to the marginal tax rate.

This brings me to the motion regarding document no. 268, which proposes splitting the Vbar bill concerning the clarification of the assessment of employment relationships and the legal presumption. I would advise against this. The proposal is currently before the Council of State. We will come back to it shortly. I would explicitly like to retain the scope to assess what is sensible at that time, but I would like to add at the same time that I have naturally listened carefully to what this House has contributed during the debate. I will, of course, weigh that up when we start working on the bill.

Chair, support for this request for a debate. I would like to suggest scheduling it for the last week before the May recess. That would give the Cabinet sufficient time. I would also like the Chair of the Ministerial Committee to attend the debate. That is Prime Minister Schoof.

This is one of the budget cuts I have to implement. It is not clear whether the regulation is effective for that purpose. At present, particularly within public schools, there are other ways to educate students about religious movements and directions in a broad sense, for example through citizenship education. To implement the change, a legislative amendment is ultimately required. Mr. Ceder is absolutely right about that.

So that will certainly come up again. I will check if there was anything else where we discussed civil debt collection. We discussed traffic fines and the customized approach of the CJB. We also discussed the reduction of collection costs. I will come back to that. Ms. Rikkers asked two further questions that I would like to address. One concerns the order of allocation, or the imputation rule. I announce that we will devote detailed attention to this in the announced letter to Parliament, which will be sent to you before May 1st. In it, we will provide a reflection on the operation of that imputation rule and the consequences of any potential adjustment thereof. Ms. Rikkers also asked whether it is possible to move towards a single collection process per creditor; that is, whether, if a creditor has multiple claims against the same debtor, these can be consolidated into one collection process. I see the importance of this. This can prevent multiple costs from being incurred per claim. It prevents an unnecessary escalation of costs. However, there are some snags involved. It is by no means always in the debtor's interest, as claims with the same creditor may have very different statuses. While it is desirable to arrive at a collective approach, this does not yet imply a statutory regulation requiring a creditor to pursue all their claims in a single collection process. A different arrangement may be more appropriate for one type of claim than for another. The collective element must be reflected in the collective arrangement and the collective payment plan. Only then can differentiation be applied. In that regard, I would like to be able to opt for concentration, though not for every collection process.

Unfortunately, I have seen too many images of wars, crimes, and what people can do to one another. That is presumably due to my age, I fear, and to the world around us. However, in the coming days at the European Council on Thursday and Friday morning, the Netherlands will commit itself very strongly to ensuring that the European Union speaks with one voice when it comes to ensuring a ceasefire and room for humanitarian aid. That is the line we follow, and along which we call upon Israel to abide by international law and the laws of war. That is what the Netherlands stands for, and what the Netherlands will also permanently call for attention to in the European Council. Perhaps this is the moment to give Mr. Van Baarle at least one concrete answer. I hope, at least, that he considers this concrete. He asked: what does the Prime Minister think of declaring the UN Secretary-General persona non grata? Let it be clear that the Netherlands stands firmly behind the United Nations and also behind the Secretary-General. Therefore, on the day Israel declared him persona non grata, the Minister of Foreign Affairs conveyed his serious concerns to his Israeli counterpart, Minister Katz. Additionally, the Minister publicly expressed his concerns by signing a statement with a large number of countries condemning Israel's decision and expressing broad support for the Secretary-General.

Chair, thank you. First of all, I would like to thank the Ministers for their answers, and I also thank my colleagues. Although it took us two weeks, we have remained in contact with each other in the meantime. There are many amendments, a number of which I believe will certainly improve the Act. I truly hope that this is a step towards an even better and further implementation of the reform agenda. As far as I am concerned, this cannot happen soon enough. In support, I have three motions. The House, having heard the deliberations, noting that the intention behind the Youth Act upon its introduction was primarily to make collective provisions available to young people; noting that in practice, mainly tailor-made provisions are established; considering that it is preferable to address requests for assistance in everyday life and as much as possible with collective provisions; requests the Government to map out how the emphasis within the Youth Act can be shifted more towards collective provisions rather than tailor-made provisions and to inform municipalities about existing possibilities, and proceeds to the order of the day.

Chair. A committed and reliable government requires a sound budget. France, with its sky-high deficit and social unrest, is now like a ship stranded on the beach that serves as a beacon, a warning, in the sea. We need a budget that takes into account the generations after us and, above all, a budget that has meaning for people. Moreover, managing scarce resources is more difficult than drawing from abundant sources. After all, we do not decide on money that belongs to the government, for all those billions of euros have been raised by society. For my party, the determining factor is whether this budget builds a BBB Netherlands, a Netherlands as a home in which we live together. Tax money does not fall from the sky. That money must first be earned. The excessive focus on skimming off money distracts from the value of earning money. For BBB, work and entrepreneurship should be rewarded rather than discouraged. Farmers and fishermen deserve a future in their profession instead of a buyout scheme. Middle-income earners desperately need extra support, and the overburdening of the working backbone of our society must stop. BBB is particularly impressed by the fact that stronger purchasing power enables people to choose for themselves how they spend their money. The right direction matters more to me than decimal places. At its core, this budget is about everyone being better off and ensuring that work becomes more rewarding again. It requires a balance between income and expenditure. It requires choices regarding the type of taxes we levy. But on balance, my party group states with full conviction that we are heading in the right direction, but that we are not there yet. A sound budget from a reliable government also takes sub-national authorities into account. That has already been said here earlier today. The central government should not storm through provinces and municipalities like a blind horse. Fluctuations in income can hit local authorities hard. If provinces and municipalities also want to be reliable, the central government must make that financially possible. When municipalities are heading for a rock-bottom year, something is going wrong. There is always tension in the give and take between the central government, provinces, and municipalities. My party group is realistic about that. But I advocate for more stability in the agreements. One possible line of thought we would like to suggest is to better structure financial agreements with provinces and municipalities based on the administrative terms of these authorities. Against this background, a question: how does the Minister view the idea of explicitly linking agreements regarding funding streams through the Municipal Fund and the Provincial Fund to municipal and provincial executive terms? After all, these are never dependent on interim elections, unless it concerns a municipal reorganization. A sound budget takes a realistic view of investments. We make such investments because we believe in the future of our country. If the costs of the Delta Works had been written down in advance, they would have been deemed unfeasible. It would then have been cheaper to plug the holes in the dikes on the islands of Zeeland and South Holland with patchwork: a guilder here, a guilder there. This attitude of *luctor et emergo*—I struggle and emerge victorious—also applies to the courage to push ahead with projects with uncertain estimates. Because it is good for the country and for the regions. Because it creates jobs, innovation, and livability. At the same time, my party expects sound estimates to be made step by step. This also applies to the Lely Line. The accessibility of the North requires decisive action, but it is important that the budget provides a solid foundation for construction. 3 billion is available in the budget. While the ink on the estimates is still drying, they are being sucked into a sinkhole because an additional 11 billion is required. This raises the question of why the Ministry of Finance is unable, and has been unable, to obtain the correct figures from the ministries involved. My party requests an explanation for this. It is possible that unexpected developments are not incorporated into a national budget. But how is it possible, I ask the Minister, that newspapers and other media can explain the cost increase, potential consequences, and scenarios in detail within a few days? Surely that amount of 11 billion cannot be that unexpected? Is the Minister fully aware that it is now all the more important to adhere to the agreements in the Framework Agreement and that construction of the Lely Line starts in Groningen, so that every euro invested ends up in the North from the very beginning? We all know that if we start in Amsterdam and the money runs out in Emmeloord, no one will bat an eye. But if we start in Groningen and stop in Emmeloord, all of Amsterdam will be lining the canals with banners. Chair. I began my contribution on behalf of BBB with the remark that the national budget is about people. There will be an interruption first, if the Chair thinks that is a good idea. He is in charge of that.

I was listening for a moment, because I heard Mr. Timmermans paying compliments to the State Commission. Rightly so, I think. He also aligns himself with the theme of the State Commission's target figures, also rightly so. But he also stated therein that his focus lies primarily on labor migration. And I wondered about something. He spoke in the newspaper yesterday about a change of course, I believe. Does he mean to say that this also has consequences for the GroenLinks-PvdA position on asylum migration?

However strange it may sound, my response was not a reaction to a tweet by a party group leader. My response was partly prompted by the fact that it was announced that a motion would be submitted during the debate on the budget of the Minister of the Interior with the tenor of what I spoke about earlier. That is why I referred to the Minister of the Interior. That was the reason for me. Incidentally, yesterday I responded in different terms. There, I simply stood behind the mayor, just as I also stood for the freedom of the right to demonstrate. But this was indeed the reason for me. My response was also in response to the questions from Mr. Klaver and the request from a number of you in your House for normative statements. Precisely because this has been raised in the House, I find it important to also express an opinion on this matter on behalf of the Cabinet. But I would like to repeat the words of Mr. Eerdmans. I have resolved not to respond to tweets from party group leaders. But as soon as it enters the public debate in the House, it is part of the debate and thus of the interaction between the Cabinet and the House.

Thank you. I will then conclude the section on antisemitism by stating that I spoke with Jewish organizations and a significant number of civil society organizations yesterday. It is my firm intention—I already made this known through various channels earlier this week—to enter into dialogue with various organizations based on religious or philosophical grounds in the near future. This includes Muslim organizations, but it is much broader than that. I will engage in dialogue with them regarding antisemitism, but at the same time, I will broaden the conversation to include, secondarily, everything that stands for "anti" in combination with discrimination.

Oh, that is quite a… It is not yet 11:00 PM. So I should actually still be fully sharp, with my apologies. The motion on document no. 25 is from Mr. Van Baarle and concerns the UEFA's banning of Israeli teams. I advise against this motion. There is no sports boycott against Israel. The motion on document no. 26 from Mr. Dijk of the SP concerns a cabinet vision on combating segregation. I advise against the motion, but at the same time I promise Mr. Dijk that we will look into the extent to which we can incorporate a number of aspects of it into the integration action plan following the motion on document no. 21 from the VVD. My final motion — or at least, the last motion currently before me — is the motion on document no. 29 regarding an agenda for strengthening shared values to break the culture of silence. I leave the judgment to the House. It is, however, very comprehensive. The ambition could therefore be very great. So I am looking for ways we can limit that somewhat together. I would also like to include that in the action programme requested by the VVD in motion no. 21. Mr. Chair, that concludes my motions.
